**Communism** (from [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language")  **[communis](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/communis#Latin "wikt:communis")** 'common, universal')[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallDagger2019-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) is a [political](/wiki/Political_philosophy "Political philosophy") and [economic](/wiki/Economic_ideology "Economic ideology") [ideology](/wiki/Ideology "Ideology") whose goal is the creation of a [communist society](/wiki/Communist_society "Communist society"), a [socioeconomic](/wiki/Socioeconomic "Socioeconomic") order centered on [common ownership](/wiki/Common_ownership "Common ownership") of the [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production"), distribution, and exchange that allocates products in society based on need.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Steele_p.43-5) A communist society entails the absence of [private property](/wiki/Private_property "Private property") and [social classes](/wiki/Social_class "Social class"),[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallDagger2019-1) and ultimately money[[6]](#cite_note-6) and the [state](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)").[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) Communism is a part of the broader [socialist movement](/wiki/History_of_socialism "History of socialism").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallDagger2019-1)

Communists often seek a voluntary state of [self-governance](/wiki/Self-governance "Self-governance") but disagree on the means to this end. This reflects a distinction between a [libertarian socialist](/wiki/Libertarian_socialism "Libertarian socialism") approach of [communization](/wiki/Communization "Communization"), [revolutionary spontaneity](/wiki/Revolutionary_spontaneity "Revolutionary spontaneity"), and [workers' self-management](/wiki/Workers%27_self-management "Workers' self-management"), and an [authoritarian socialist](/wiki/Authoritarian_socialism "Authoritarian socialism"), [vanguardist](/wiki/Vanguardist "Vanguardist"), or [party](/wiki/Communist_party "Communist party")-driven approach to establish a [socialist state](/wiki/Socialist_state "Socialist state"), which is expected to [wither away](/wiki/Withering_away_of_the_state "Withering away of the state").[[10]](#cite_note-Kinna_2012-10) Communist parties have been described as radical left or far-left.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch2009126–143-11)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgeWilcox199695-12)[[note 1]](#cite_note-20)

There are many [variants of communism](/wiki/Variants_of_communism "Variants of communism"), such as [anarchist communism](/wiki/Anarchist_communism "Anarchist communism"), [Marxist schools of thought](/wiki/Marxist_schools_of_thought "Marxist schools of thought") (including [Leninism](/wiki/Leninism "Leninism") and its offshoots), and [religious communism](/wiki/Religious_communism "Religious communism"). These ideologies share the analysis that the current order of society stems from the [capitalist](/wiki/Capitalist_mode_of_production_(Marxist_theory) "Capitalist mode of production (Marxist theory)") [economic system](/wiki/Economic_system "Economic system") and [mode of production](/wiki/Mode_of_production "Mode of production"); they believe that there are two major social classes under capitalism, that the relationship between them is exploitative, and that it can only be resolved through [social revolution](/wiki/Social_revolution "Social revolution").[[20]](#cite_note-Marx_&_Engels_1848-21)[[note 2]](#cite_note-23) The two classes under capitalism are the [proletariat](/wiki/Proletariat "Proletariat") ([working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class")), who make up most of the population and sell their [labor power](/wiki/Labour_power "Labour power") to survive, and the [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie") (owning class), a minority that derives profit from employing the proletariat through private ownership of the means of production.[[20]](#cite_note-Marx_&_Engels_1848-21) Some variants additionally emphasize [feudal](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism") classes, such as the [peasantry](/wiki/Peasant "Peasant") and [feudal lords](/wiki/Feudal_lord "Feudal lord"), or other classes. According to this, a [communist revolution](/wiki/Communist_revolution "Communist revolution") would put the working class in power,[[22]](#cite_note-24) and establish common ownership of property, the primary element in the transformation of society towards a [socialist mode of production](/wiki/Socialist_mode_of_production "Socialist mode of production").[[23]](#cite_note-Steele_1992,_pp._44–45-25)[[24]](#cite_note-Gregory_&_Stuart_2003,_p._118-26)[[25]](#cite_note-Bockman_2011,_p._20-27)

Communism in its modern form grew out of the socialist movement in 19th-century Europe that argued capitalism caused the misery of urban factory workers.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallDagger2019-1) In 1848, [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") and [Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels") offered a new definition of communism in *[The Communist Manifesto](/wiki/The_Communist_Manifesto "The Communist Manifesto")*. In the 20th century, [Communist governments](/wiki/Communist_state "Communist state") espousing [Marxism–Leninism](/wiki/Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism "Marxism–Leninism") came to power,[[26]](#cite_note-28)[[note 3]](#cite_note-34) first in the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") with the 1917 [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution"), then in Eastern Europe, Asia, and other regions after World War II.[[32]](#cite_note-35) By the 1920s, communism had become one of the two dominant [types of socialism](/wiki/Types_of_socialism "Types of socialism") in the world, the other being [social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy").[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman20055-36)

For much of the 20th century, more than one-third of the world's population lived under Communist governments. These were characterized by [one-party rule](/wiki/One-party_rule "One-party rule"), rejection of private property and capitalism, state control of economic activity and [mass media](/wiki/Mass_media "Mass media"), [restrictions on](/wiki/State_atheism "State atheism") [freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion"), and suppression of opposition. With the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union") in 1991, many governments abolished Communist rule.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallDagger2019-1)[[34]](#cite_note-37)[[35]](#cite_note-Dunn-38) Only a few nominally Communist governments remain, such as [China](/wiki/China "China"),[[36]](#cite_note-39) [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"), [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos"), [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea"),[[note 4]](#cite_note-NKorea-46) and [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam").[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELansford20079–24,_36–44-47) Except for North Korea, these have allowed more economic competition while maintaining one-party rule.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallDagger2019-1) Communism's decline has been attributed to economic inefficiency and to [authoritarianism](/wiki/Authoritarianism "Authoritarianism") and [bureaucracy](/wiki/Bureaucracy "Bureaucracy") within Communist governments.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallDagger2019-1)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELansford20079–24,_36–44-47)[[44]](#cite_note-:0-48)

Although the emergence of the Soviet Union as the first state based on Marxism–Leninism led to socialism becoming widely associated with the [Soviet economic model](/wiki/Soviet_economic_model "Soviet economic model"), several scholars argue that in practice this model functioned as a form of [state capitalism](/wiki/State_capitalism "State capitalism").[[45]](#cite_note-Chomsky,_Howard,_Fitzgibbons-49)[[46]](#cite_note-50) Public memory of 20th-century Marxist-Leninist states has been described as a battleground between [anti-communism](/wiki/Anti-communism "Anti-communism") and [anti anti-communism](/wiki/Anti_anti-communism "Anti anti-communism").[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGhodseeSehonDresser2018-51) Authors have written about [mass killings under communist regimes](/wiki/Mass_killings_under_communist_regimes "Mass killings under communist regimes") and [mortality rates](/wiki/Mortality_rate "Mortality rate"),[[note 5]](#cite_note-third-65)[[note 6]](#cite_note-fourth-70) which remain controversial, polarized, and debated topics in academia, historiography, and politics when discussing communism and the legacy of Marxist-Leninist states.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGhodsee2014-71)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeumayer2018-72)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] From the 1990s, many Communist parties adopted democratic principles and came to share power with others in government, such as the [CPN UML](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Nepal_(Unified_Marxist%E2%80%93Leninist) "Communist Party of Nepal (Unified Marxist–Leninist)") and the [Nepal Communist Party](/wiki/Nepal_Communist_Party "Nepal Communist Party"), which support [People's Multiparty Democracy](/wiki/People%27s_Multiparty_Democracy "People's Multiparty Democracy") in [Nepal](/wiki/Communism_in_Nepal "Communism in Nepal").[[67]](#cite_note-Peter-73)[[68]](#cite_note-The_Economist-74)[[69]](#cite_note-The_Diplomat-75)

## Etymology and terminology

*Communism* derives from the [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") word *communisme*, a combination of the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") word *communis* (which literally means *common*) and the suffix *‑isme* (an act, practice, or process of doing something).[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarper2020-76)[[71]](#cite_note-77) Semantically, *communis* can be translated to "of or for the community", while *isme* is a suffix that indicates the abstraction into a state, condition, action, or [doctrine](/wiki/Doctrine "Doctrine"). *Communism* may be interpreted as "the state of being of or for the community"; this semantic constitution has led to numerous usages of the word in its evolution. Prior to becoming associated with its more modern conception of an economic and political organization, it was initially used to designate various social situations. After 1848, *communism* came to be primarily associated with [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism"), most specifically embodied in *[The Communist Manifesto](/wiki/The_Communist_Manifesto "The Communist Manifesto")*, which proposed a particular type of communism.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallDagger2019-1)[[72]](#cite_note-Morris-78)

One of the first uses of the word in its modern sense is in a letter sent by [Victor d'Hupay](/wiki/Victor_d%27Hupay "Victor d'Hupay") to [Nicolas Restif de la Bretonne](/wiki/Nicolas_Restif_de_la_Bretonne "Nicolas Restif de la Bretonne") around 1785, in which d'Hupay describes himself as an *auteur communiste* ("communist author").[[73]](#cite_note-79) In 1793, [Restif](/wiki/Nicolas_Restif_de_la_Bretonne "Nicolas Restif de la Bretonne") first used *communisme* to describe a social order based on egalitarianism and the common ownership of property.[[74]](#cite_note-Hodges-80) Restif would go on to use the term frequently in his writing and was the first to describe communism as a [form of government](/wiki/Government#Forms "Government").[[75]](#cite_note-81) [John Goodwyn Barmby](/wiki/John_Goodwyn_Barmby "John Goodwyn Barmby") is credited with the first use of *communism* in English, around 1840.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarper2020-76)

### Communism and socialism

Since the 1840s, the term *communism* has usually been distinguished from *[socialism](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism")*. The modern definition and usage of the term *socialism* was settled by the 1860s, becoming predominant over alternative terms such as *associationism* ([Fourierism](/wiki/Fourierism "Fourierism")), *[mutualism](/wiki/Mutualism_(economic_theory) "Mutualism (economic theory)")*, or *[co-operative](/wiki/Co-operative "Co-operative")*, which had previously been used as synonyms. Meanwhile, the term *communism* fell out of use during this period.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams1983[httpsarchiveorgdetailskeywordsvocabula00willrichpage289_289]-82)

An early distinction between *communism* and *socialism* was that the latter aimed to only socialize [production](/wiki/Production_(economics) "Production (economics)"), whereas the former aimed to socialize both production and [consumption](/wiki/Consumption_(economics) "Consumption (economics)") (in the form of common access to [final goods](/wiki/Final_good "Final good")).[[5]](#cite_note-Steele_p.43-5) This distinction can be observed in Marx's communism, where the distribution of products is based on the principle of "[to each according to his needs](/wiki/To_each_according_to_his_needs "To each according to his needs")", in contrast to a socialist principle of "[to each according to his contribution](/wiki/To_each_according_to_his_contribution "To each according to his contribution")".[[24]](#cite_note-Gregory_&_Stuart_2003,_p._118-26) Socialism has been described as a philosophy seeking distributive justice, and communism as a subset of socialism that prefers economic equality as its form of distributive justice.[[77]](#cite_note-83)

In 19th century Europe, the use of the terms *communism* and *socialism* eventually accorded with the cultural attitude of adherents and opponents towards [religion](/wiki/Religion "Religion"). In European [Christendom](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom"), *communism* was believed to be the [atheist](/wiki/Atheist "Atheist") way of life. In [Protestant England](/wiki/Protestant_England "Protestant England"), *communism* was too [phonetically](/wiki/Phonetically "Phonetically") similar to the Roman Catholic *[communion rite](/wiki/Communion_rite "Communion rite")*, hence English atheists denoted themselves socialists.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams1983[httpsarchiveorgdetailskeywordsvocabula00willrichpage289_289]-82) [Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels") stated that in 1848, at the time when *[The Communist Manifesto](/wiki/The_Communist_Manifesto "The Communist Manifesto")* was first published,[[78]](#cite_note-84) socialism was respectable on the continent, while communism was not; the [Owenites](/wiki/Owenism "Owenism") in England and the Fourierists in France were considered respectable socialists, while working-class movements that "proclaimed the necessity of total social change" denoted themselves *communists*. This latter branch of socialism produced the communist work of [Étienne Cabet](/wiki/%C3%89tienne_Cabet "Étienne Cabet") in France and [Wilhelm Weitling](/wiki/Wilhelm_Weitling "Wilhelm Weitling") in Germany.[[79]](#cite_note-85) While [liberal democrats](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy") looked to the [Revolutions of 1848](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848 "Revolutions of 1848") as a [democratic revolution](/wiki/Democratic_revolution "Democratic revolution"), which in the long run ensured [liberty, equality, and fraternity](/wiki/Liberty,_equality,_and_fraternity "Liberty, equality, and fraternity"), Marxists denounced 1848 as a betrayal of working-class ideals by a [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie") indifferent to the legitimate demands of the [proletariat](/wiki/Proletariat "Proletariat").[[80]](#cite_note-86)

By 1888, Marxists employed the term *socialism* in place of *communism*, which had come to be considered an old-fashioned synonym for the former. It was not until 1917, with the [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution"), that *socialism* came to be used to refer to a distinct stage between [capitalism](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism") and communism. This intermediate stage was a concept introduced by [Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin") as a means to defend the [Bolshevik](/wiki/Bolshevik "Bolshevik") seizure of power against traditional Marxist criticism that Russia's [productive forces](/wiki/Productive_forces "Productive forces") were not sufficiently developed for [socialist revolution](/wiki/Socialist_revolution "Socialist revolution").[[23]](#cite_note-Steele_1992,_pp._44–45-25) A distinction between *communist* and *socialist* as descriptors of political ideologies arose in 1918 after the [Russian Social Democratic Labour Party](/wiki/Russian_Social_Democratic_Labour_Party "Russian Social Democratic Labour Party") renamed itself as the [Communist Party of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Communist Party of the Soviet Union"), which resulted in the adjective *Communist* being used to refer to socialists who supported the politics and theories of Bolshevism, [Leninism](/wiki/Leninism "Leninism"), and later in the 1920s those of [Marxism–Leninism](/wiki/Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism "Marxism–Leninism").[[81]](#cite_note-87) In spite of this common usage, [Communist parties](/wiki/Communist_parties "Communist parties") also continued to describe themselves as socialists dedicated to socialism.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams1983[httpsarchiveorgdetailskeywordsvocabula00willrichpage289_289]-82)

According to *The Oxford Handbook of Karl Marx*, "Marx used many terms to refer to a post-capitalist society – positive humanism, socialism, Communism, realm of free individuality, free association of producers, etc. He used these terms completely interchangeably. The notion that 'socialism' and 'Communism' are distinct historical stages is alien to his work and only entered the lexicon of Marxism after his death."[[82]](#cite_note-Hudis_et_al._2018-88) According to the *[Encyclopædia Britannica](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica "Encyclopædia Britannica")*, "Exactly how communism differs from socialism has long been a matter of debate, but the distinction rests largely on the communists' adherence to the revolutionary socialism of Karl Marx."[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallDagger2019-1)

### Associated usage and Communist states

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Hammer_and_Sickle_and_Star.svg/250px-Hammer_and_Sickle_and_Star.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Hammer_and_Sickle_and_Star.svg)

The [hammer and sickle](/wiki/Hammer_and_sickle "Hammer and sickle") is a common theme of [communist symbolism](/wiki/Communist_symbolism "Communist symbolism"). This is an example of a hammer and sickle and [red star](/wiki/Red_star "Red star") design from the [flag of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Flag_of_the_Soviet_Union "Flag of the Soviet Union").

In the United States, *communism* is widely used as a pejorative term as part of a [Red Scare](/wiki/Red_Scare "Red Scare"), much like *socialism*, and mainly in reference to [authoritarian socialism](/wiki/Authoritarian_socialism "Authoritarian socialism") and [Communist states](/wiki/Communist_state "Communist state"). The emergence of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") as the world's first Communist state led to the term's widespread association with Marxism–Leninist and the [Soviet-type economic planning](/wiki/Soviet-type_economic_planning "Soviet-type economic planning") model.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallDagger2019-1)[[83]](#cite_note-89)[[84]](#cite_note-90) In his essay "Judging Nazism and Communism",[[85]](#cite_note-91) [Martin Malia](/wiki/Martin_Malia "Martin Malia") defines a "generic Communism" category as any Communist [political party](/wiki/Political_party "Political party") movement led by [intellectuals](/wiki/Intellectual "Intellectual"); this [umbrella term](/wiki/Umbrella_term "Umbrella term") allows grouping together such different [regimes](/wiki/Regime "Regime") as radical [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet "Soviet") industrialism and the [Khmer Rouge](/wiki/Khmer_Rouge "Khmer Rouge")'s anti-urbanism.[[86]](#cite_note-David-Fox_2004-92) According to [Alexander Dallin](/wiki/Alexander_Dallin "Alexander Dallin"), the idea to group together different countries, such as [Afghanistan](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_Afghanistan "Democratic Republic of Afghanistan") and [Hungary](/wiki/Hungarian_People%27s_Republic "Hungarian People's Republic"), has no adequate explanation.[[87]](#cite_note-Dallin_2000-93)

While the term *Communist state* is used by Western historians, political scientists, and news media to refer to countries ruled by Communist parties, these [socialist states](/wiki/List_of_non-communist_socialist_states "List of non-communist socialist states") themselves did not describe themselves as communist or claim to have achieved communism; they referred to themselves as being a socialist state that is in the process of constructing communism.[[88]](#cite_note-94) Terms used by Communist states include *[national-democratic](/wiki/National-democratic_state "National-democratic state")*, *[people's democratic](/wiki/People%27s_democracy_(Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism) "People's democracy (Marxism–Leninism)")*, *[socialist-oriented](/wiki/Socialist-leaning_countries "Socialist-leaning countries")*, and *[workers and peasants'](/wiki/Socialist_state "Socialist state")* states.[[89]](#cite_note-95)

## History

Main article: [History of communism](/wiki/History_of_communism "History of communism")

### Early communism

Further information: [Pre-Marxist communism](/wiki/Pre-Marxist_communism "Pre-Marxist communism"), [Primitive communism](/wiki/Primitive_communism "Primitive communism"), [Religious communism](/wiki/Religious_communism "Religious communism"), [Scientific socialism](/wiki/Scientific_socialism "Scientific socialism"), and [Utopian socialism](/wiki/Utopian_socialism "Utopian socialism")

According to [Richard Pipes](/wiki/Richard_Pipes "Richard Pipes"),[[90]](#cite_note-Pipes-96) the idea of a [classless](/wiki/Classless_society "Classless society"), [egalitarian](/wiki/Egalitarian "Egalitarian") society first emerged in [ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"). Since the 20th century, [ancient Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") has been examined in this context, as well as thinkers such as [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle"), [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero"), [Demosthenes](/wiki/Demosthenes "Demosthenes"), [Plato](/wiki/Plato "Plato"), and [Tacitus](/wiki/Tacitus "Tacitus"). Plato, in particular, has been considered as a possible communist or socialist theorist,[[91]](#cite_note-97) or as the first author to give communism a serious consideration.[[92]](#cite_note-98) The 5th-century [Mazdak](/wiki/Mazdak "Mazdak") movement in [Persia](/wiki/Persia "Persia") (modern-day Iran) has been described as *communistic* for challenging the enormous privileges of the [noble classes](/wiki/Noble_class "Noble class") and the [clergy](/wiki/Clergy "Clergy"), criticizing the institution of [private property](/wiki/Private_property "Private property"), and striving to create an egalitarian society.[[93]](#cite_note-99)[[94]](#cite_note-Ermak_2019-100) At one time or another, various small communist communities existed, generally under the inspiration of [religious text](/wiki/Religious_text "Religious text").[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELansford200724–25-54)

In the medieval [Christian Church](/wiki/Christian_Church "Christian Church"), some [monastic](/wiki/Monastic "Monastic") communities and [religious orders](/wiki/Religious_order "Religious order") shared their land and their other property. Sects deemed heretical such as the [Waldensians](/wiki/Waldensians "Waldensians") preached an early form of [Christian communism](/wiki/Christian_communism "Christian communism").[[95]](#cite_note-Busky_2002_p._33-101)[[96]](#cite_note-Boer_2019_p._12-102) As summarized by historians Janzen Rod and Max Stanton, the [Hutterites](/wiki/Hutterites "Hutterites") believed in strict adherence to biblical principles, church discipline, and practised a form of communism. In their words, the Hutterites "established in their communities a rigorous system of Ordnungen, which were codes of rules and regulations that governed all aspects of life and ensured a unified perspective. As an economic system, communism was attractive to many of the peasants who supported social revolution in sixteenth century central Europe."[[97]](#cite_note-103) This link was highlighted in one of Marx's early writings; Marx stated that "[a]s Christ is the intermediary unto whom man unburdens all his divinity, all his religious bonds, so the state is the mediator unto which he transfers all his Godlessness, all his human liberty."[[98]](#cite_note-104) [Thomas Müntzer](/wiki/Thomas_M%C3%BCntzer "Thomas Müntzer") led a large [Anabaptist](/wiki/Anabaptist "Anabaptist") communist movement during the [German Peasants' War](/wiki/German_Peasants%27_War "German Peasants' War"), which [Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels") analyzed in his 1850 work *[The Peasant War in Germany](/wiki/The_Peasant_War_in_Germany "The Peasant War in Germany")*. The [Marxist](/wiki/Marxist "Marxist") communist ethos that aims for unity reflects the [Christian universalist](/wiki/Christian_universalist "Christian universalist") teaching that humankind is one and that there is only one god who does not discriminate among people.[[99]](#cite_note-105)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Hans_Holbein%2C_the_Younger_-_Sir_Thomas_More_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Hans_Holbein%2C_the_Younger_-_Sir_Thomas_More_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hans_Holbein,_the_Younger_-_Sir_Thomas_More_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

[Thomas More](/wiki/Thomas_More "Thomas More"), whose *[Utopia](/wiki/Utopia_(More_book) "Utopia (More book)")* portrayed a society based on common ownership of property

Communist thought has also been traced back to the works of the 16th-century English writer [Thomas More](/wiki/Thomas_More "Thomas More").[[100]](#cite_note-106) In his 1516 [treatise](/wiki/Treatise "Treatise") titled *[Utopia](/wiki/Utopia_(More_book) "Utopia (More book)")*, More portrayed a society based on [common ownership](/wiki/Common_ownership "Common ownership") of property, whose rulers administered it through the application of [reason](/wiki/Reason "Reason") and [virtue](/wiki/Virtue "Virtue").[[101]](#cite_note-107) Marxist communist theoretician [Karl Kautsky](/wiki/Karl_Kautsky "Karl Kautsky"), who popularized Marxist communism in Western Europe more than any other thinker apart from Engels, published *Thomas More and His Utopia*, a work about More, whose ideas could be regarded as "the foregleam of Modern Socialism" according to Kautsky. During the [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution") in Russia, [Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin") suggested that a monument be dedicated to More, alongside other important Western thinkers.[[102]](#cite_note-Papke_2016-108)

In the 17th century, communist thought surfaced again in England, where a [Puritan](/wiki/Puritan "Puritan") religious group known as the [Diggers](/wiki/Diggers "Diggers") advocated the abolition of private ownership of land. In his 1895 *Cromwell and Communism*,[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBernstein1895-109) [Eduard Bernstein](/wiki/Eduard_Bernstein "Eduard Bernstein") stated that several groups during the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") (especially the Diggers) espoused clear communistic, [agrarianist](/wiki/Agrarianist "Agrarianist") ideals and that [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell")'s attitude towards these groups was at best ambivalent and often hostile.[[104]](#cite_note-110)[[105]](#cite_note-111) Criticism of the idea of private property continued into the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") of the 18th century through such thinkers as [Gabriel Bonnot de Mably](/wiki/Gabriel_Bonnot_de_Mably "Gabriel Bonnot de Mably"), [Jean Meslier](/wiki/Jean_Meslier "Jean Meslier"), [Étienne-Gabriel Morelly](/wiki/%C3%89tienne-Gabriel_Morelly "Étienne-Gabriel Morelly"), and [Jean-Jacques Rousseau](/wiki/Jean-Jacques_Rousseau "Jean-Jacques Rousseau") in France.[[106]](#cite_note-112) During the upheaval of the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), communism emerged as a political doctrine under the auspices of [François-Noël Babeuf](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois-No%C3%ABl_Babeuf "François-Noël Babeuf"), [Nicolas Restif de la Bretonne](/wiki/Nicolas_Restif_de_la_Bretonne "Nicolas Restif de la Bretonne"), and [Sylvain Maréchal](/wiki/Sylvain_Mar%C3%A9chal "Sylvain Maréchal"), all of whom can be considered the progenitors of modern communism, according to [James H. Billington](/wiki/James_H._Billington "James H. Billington").[[107]](#cite_note-Billington-113)

In the early 19th century, various social reformers founded communities based on common ownership. Unlike many previous communist communities, they replaced the religious emphasis with a rational and philanthropic basis.[[108]](#cite_note-britannica-114) Notable among them were [Robert Owen](/wiki/Robert_Owen "Robert Owen"), who founded [New Harmony, Indiana](/wiki/New_Harmony,_Indiana "New Harmony, Indiana"), in 1825, and [Charles Fourier](/wiki/Charles_Fourier "Charles Fourier"), whose followers organized other settlements in the United States, such as [Brook Farm](/wiki/Brook_Farm "Brook Farm") in 1841.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallDagger2019-1) In its modern form, communism grew out of the [socialist movement](/wiki/History_of_socialism "History of socialism") in 19th-century Europe. As the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") advanced, socialist critics blamed capitalism for the misery of the [proletariat](/wiki/Proletariat "Proletariat") – a new class of urban factory workers who labored under often-hazardous conditions. Foremost among these critics were Marx and his associate Engels. In 1848, Marx and Engels offered a new definition of communism and popularized the term in their famous pamphlet *[The Communist Manifesto](/wiki/The_Communist_Manifesto "The Communist Manifesto")*.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallDagger2019-1)

### Revolutionary wave of 1917–1923

Further information: [Revolutions of 1917–1923](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1917%E2%80%931923 "Revolutions of 1917–1923")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Lenin_in_1920_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Lenin_in_1920_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lenin_in_1920_(cropped).jpg)

[Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin"), founder of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") and the leader of the [Bolshevik party](/wiki/Bolshevik_party "Bolshevik party")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Leon_Trotsky_%283x4_cropped%29.jpg/250px-Leon_Trotsky_%283x4_cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leon_Trotsky_(3x4_cropped).jpg)

[Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky"), founder of the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") and a key figure in the [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution")

In 1917, the [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution") in Russia set the conditions for the rise to state power of [Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin")'s [Bolsheviks](/wiki/Bolsheviks "Bolsheviks"), which was the first time any avowedly communist party reached that position. The revolution transferred power to the [All-Russian Congress of Soviets](/wiki/All-Russian_Congress_of_Soviets "All-Russian Congress of Soviets") in which the Bolsheviks had a majority.[[109]](#cite_note-115)[[110]](#cite_note-116)[[111]](#cite_note-117) The event generated a great deal of practical and theoretical debate within the Marxist movement, as Marx stated that socialism and communism would be built upon foundations laid by the most advanced capitalist development; however, the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") was one of the poorest countries in Europe with an enormous, largely illiterate peasantry, and a minority of industrial workers. Marx warned against attempts "to transform my historical sketch of the genesis of capitalism in Western Europe into a historico-philosophy theory of the *arche générale* imposed by fate upon every people, whatever the historic circumstances in which it finds itself",[[112]](#cite_note-118) and stated that Russia might be able to skip the stage of bourgeois rule through the *[Obshchina](/wiki/Obshchina "Obshchina")*.[[113]](#cite_note-119)[[note 7]](#cite_note-123) The moderate [Mensheviks](/wiki/Mensheviks "Mensheviks") (minority) opposed Lenin's Bolsheviks (majority) plan for [socialist revolution](/wiki/Socialist_revolution "Socialist revolution") before the [capitalist mode of production](/wiki/Capitalist_mode_of_production_(Marxist_theory) "Capitalist mode of production (Marxist theory)") was more fully developed. The Bolsheviks' successful rise to power was based upon the slogans such as "Peace, Bread, and Land", which tapped into the massive public desire for an end to Russian involvement in [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the peasants' demand for [land reform](/wiki/Land_reform "Land reform"), and popular support for the [soviets](/wiki/Soviet_(council) "Soviet (council)").[[117]](#cite_note-124) 50,000 workers had passed a resolution in favour of Bolshevik demand for transfer of power to the [soviets](/wiki/Soviets "Soviets")[[118]](#cite_note-125)[[119]](#cite_note-126) Lenin's government also instituted a number of progressive measures such as [universal education](/wiki/Universal_access_to_education "Universal access to education"), [healthcare](/wiki/Universal_healthcare "Universal healthcare") and [equal rights for women](/wiki/Women_in_Russia "Women in Russia").[[120]](#cite_note-127)[[121]](#cite_note-128)[[122]](#cite_note-129) The initial stage of the October Revolution which involved the assault on [Petrograd](/wiki/Petrograd "Petrograd") occurred largely without any human [casualties](/wiki/Casualty_(person) "Casualty (person)").[[123]](#cite_note-130)[[124]](#cite_note-131)[[125]](#cite_note-132)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

By November 1917, the [Russian Provisional Government](/wiki/Russian_Provisional_Government "Russian Provisional Government") had been widely discredited by its failure to withdraw from World War I, implement land reform, or convene the [Russian Constituent Assembly](/wiki/Russian_Constituent_Assembly "Russian Constituent Assembly") to draft a constitution, leaving the soviets in *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* control of the country. The Bolsheviks moved to hand power to the [Second All-Russian Congress of Soviets of Workers' and Soldiers' Deputies](/wiki/Second_All-Russian_Congress_of_Soviets_of_Workers%27_and_Soldiers%27_Deputies "Second All-Russian Congress of Soviets of Workers' and Soldiers' Deputies") in the October Revolution; after a few weeks of deliberation, the [Left Socialist-Revolutionaries](/wiki/Left_Socialist-Revolutionaries "Left Socialist-Revolutionaries") formed a [coalition government](/wiki/Coalition_government "Coalition government") with the Bolsheviks from November 1917 to July 1918, while the right-wing faction of the [Socialist Revolutionary Party](/wiki/Socialist_Revolutionary_Party "Socialist Revolutionary Party") boycotted the soviets and denounced the October Revolution as an illegal [coup](/wiki/Coup "Coup"). In the [1917 Russian Constituent Assembly election](/wiki/1917_Russian_Constituent_Assembly_election "1917 Russian Constituent Assembly election"), socialist parties totaled well over 70% of the vote. The Bolsheviks were clear winners in the urban centres, and took around two-thirds of the votes of soldiers on the Western Front, obtaining 23.3% of the vote; the Socialist Revolutionaries finished first on the strength of support from the country's rural peasantry, who were for the most part [single issue voters](/wiki/Single-issue_politics "Single-issue politics"), that issue being land reform, obtaining 37.6%, while the Ukrainian Socialist Bloc finished a distant third at 12.7%, and the Mensheviks obtained a disappointing fourth place at 3.0%.[[126]](#cite_note-Dando_1966-133)

Most of the Socialist Revolutionary Party's seats went to the right-wing faction. Citing outdated voter-rolls, which did not acknowledge the party split, and the assembly's conflicts with the Congress of Soviets, the Bolshevik–Left Socialist-Revolutionaries government moved to dissolve the Constituent Assembly in January 1918. The Draft Decree on the Dissolution of the Constituent Assembly was issued by the [Central Executive Committee of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Central_Executive_Committee_of_the_Soviet_Union "Central Executive Committee of the Soviet Union"), a committee dominated by Lenin, who had previously supported a [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system") of free elections. After the Bolshevik defeat, Lenin started referring to the assembly as a "deceptive form of bourgeois-democratic parliamentarianism."[[126]](#cite_note-Dando_1966-133) Some argued this was the beginning of the development of [vanguardism](/wiki/Vanguardism "Vanguardism") as an hierarchical party–elite that controls society,[[127]](#cite_note-134) which resulted in a split between [anarchism and Marxism](/wiki/Anarchism_and_Marxism "Anarchism and Marxism"), and [Leninist](/wiki/Leninist "Leninist") communism assuming the dominant position for most of the 20th century, excluding rival socialist currents.[[128]](#cite_note-135)

Other communists and Marxists, especially [social democrats](/wiki/Social_democrats "Social democrats") who favored the development of [liberal democracy](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy") as a prerequisite to [socialism](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism"), were critical of the Bolsheviks from the beginning due to Russia being seen as too backward for a [socialist revolution](/wiki/Socialist_revolution "Socialist revolution").[[23]](#cite_note-Steele_1992,_pp._44–45-25) [Council communism](/wiki/Council_communism "Council communism") and [left communism](/wiki/Left_communism "Left communism"), inspired by the [German Revolution of 1918–1919](/wiki/German_Revolution_of_1918%E2%80%931919 "German Revolution of 1918–1919") and the wide [proletarian revolutionary](/wiki/Proletarian_revolution "Proletarian revolution") wave, arose in response to developments in Russia and are critical of self-declared constitutionally [socialist states](/wiki/Socialist_state "Socialist state"). Some left-wing parties, such as the [Socialist Party of Great Britain](/wiki/Socialist_Party_of_Great_Britain "Socialist Party of Great Britain"), boasted of having called the Bolsheviks, and by extension those [Communist states](/wiki/Communist_state "Communist state") which either followed or were inspired by the Soviet Bolshevik model of development, establishing [state capitalism](/wiki/State_capitalism "State capitalism") in late 1917, as would be described during the 20th century by several academics, economists, and other scholars,[[45]](#cite_note-Chomsky,_Howard,_Fitzgibbons-49) or a [command economy](/wiki/Command_economy "Command economy").[[129]](#cite_note-The_Soviet_Union_Has_an_Administered,_Not_a_Planned,_Economy,_1985-136)[[130]](#cite_note-Gregory_2004-137)[[131]](#cite_note-Ellman_2007-138) Before the Soviet path of development became known as *socialism*, in reference to the [two-stage theory](/wiki/Two-stage_theory "Two-stage theory"), communists made no major distinction between the [socialist mode of production](/wiki/Socialist_mode_of_production "Socialist mode of production") and communism;[[82]](#cite_note-Hudis_et_al._2018-88) it is consistent with, and helped to inform, early concepts of socialism in which the [law of value](/wiki/Law_of_value "Law of value") no longer directs economic activity. Monetary relations in the form of [exchange-value](/wiki/Exchange_value "Exchange value"), [profit](/wiki/Profit_(economics) "Profit (economics)"), [interest](/wiki/Interest "Interest"), and [wage labor](/wiki/Wage_labour "Wage labour") would not operate and apply to Marxist socialism.[[25]](#cite_note-Bockman_2011,_p._20-27)

[![One of the founders of the Italian Communist Party, one of the greatest interpreters of Marxism, a martyr of anti-fascism, and perhaps the most important Italian intellectual.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Gramsci.png/250px-Gramsci.png)](/wiki/File:Gramsci.png)

[Antonio Gramsci](/wiki/Antonio_Gramsci "Antonio Gramsci") (1891-1937) One of the founders of the [Italian Communist Party](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party"), one of the greatest interpreters of Marxism, a martyr of [anti-fascism](/wiki/Anti-fascism "Anti-fascism"), and perhaps the most important Italian intellectual.

While [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin") stated that the law of value would still apply to socialism and that the Soviet Union was *socialist* under this new definition, which was followed by other Communist leaders, many other communists maintain the original definition and state that Communist states never established socialism in this sense. Lenin described his policies as state capitalism but saw them as necessary for the development of socialism, which left-wing critics say was never established, while some [Marxist–Leninists](/wiki/Marxist%E2%80%93Leninists "Marxist–Leninists") state that it was established only during the [Stalin era](/wiki/Stalin_era "Stalin era") and [Mao era](/wiki/Mao_era "Mao era"), and then became capitalist states ruled by *[revisionists](/wiki/Revisionism_(Marxism) "Revisionism (Marxism)")*; others state that Maoist China was always state capitalist, and uphold [People's Socialist Republic of Albania](/wiki/People%27s_Socialist_Republic_of_Albania "People's Socialist Republic of Albania") as the only [socialist state](/wiki/Socialist_state "Socialist state") after the Soviet Union under Stalin,[[132]](#cite_note-Bland_1995-139)[[133]](#cite_note-Bland_1997-140) who first stated to have achieved *socialism* with the [1936 Constitution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/1936_Constitution_of_the_Soviet_Union "1936 Constitution of the Soviet Union").[[134]](#cite_note-141)

### Communist states

#### Soviet Union

Further information: [Communist state](/wiki/Communist_state "Communist state") and [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union")

[War communism](/wiki/War_communism "War communism") was the first system adopted by the Bolsheviks during the [Russian Civil War](/wiki/Russian_Civil_War "Russian Civil War") as a result of the many challenges.[[135]](#cite_note-Peters_1998-142) Despite *communism* in the name, it had nothing to do with communism, with strict discipline for workers, [strike actions](/wiki/Strike_action "Strike action") forbidden, obligatory labor duty, and military-style control, and has been described as simple [authoritarian](/wiki/Authoritarian "Authoritarian") control by the Bolsheviks to maintain power and control in the Soviet regions, rather than any coherent political [ideology](/wiki/Ideology "Ideology").[[136]](#cite_note-143) The Soviet Union was established in 1922. Before the [broad ban](/wiki/Ban_on_factions_in_the_Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Ban on factions in the Communist Party of the Soviet Union") in 1921, there were several factions in the Communist party, more prominently among them the [Left Opposition](/wiki/Left_Opposition "Left Opposition"), the [Right Opposition](/wiki/Right_Opposition "Right Opposition"), and the [Workers' Opposition](/wiki/Workers%27_Opposition "Workers' Opposition"), which debated on the path of development to follow. The Left and Workers' oppositions were more critical of the state-capitalist development and the Workers' in particular was critical of [bureaucratization](/wiki/Bureaucratization "Bureaucratization") and development from above, while the Right Opposition was more supporting of state-capitalist development and advocated the [New Economic Policy](/wiki/New_Economic_Policy "New Economic Policy").[[135]](#cite_note-Peters_1998-142) Following Lenin's [democratic centralism](/wiki/Democratic_centralism "Democratic centralism"), the Leninist parties were organized on a hierarchical basis, with active cells of members as the broad base. They were made up only of elite [cadres](/wiki/Cadre_(politics) "Cadre (politics)") approved by higher members of the party as being reliable and completely subject to [party discipline](/wiki/Party_discipline "Party discipline").[[137]](#cite_note-World_War_II_2001-144) [Trotskyism](/wiki/Trotskyism "Trotskyism") overtook the left communists as the main dissident communist current, while more [libertarian communisms](/wiki/Libertarian_communism "Libertarian communism"), dating back to the [libertarian Marxist](/wiki/Libertarian_Marxist "Libertarian Marxist") current of council communism, remained important dissident communisms outside the Soviet Union. The [Great Purge](/wiki/Great_Purge "Great Purge") of 1936–1938 was [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin")'s attempt to destroy any possible opposition within the [Communist Party of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Communist Party of the Soviet Union"). In the [Moscow trials](/wiki/Moscow_trials "Moscow trials"), many old Bolsheviks who had played prominent roles during the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution") or in Lenin's Soviet government afterwards, including [Lev Kamenev](/wiki/Lev_Kamenev "Lev Kamenev"), [Grigory Zinoviev](/wiki/Grigory_Zinoviev "Grigory Zinoviev"), [Alexei Rykov](/wiki/Alexei_Rykov "Alexei Rykov"), and [Nikolai Bukharin](/wiki/Nikolai_Bukharin "Nikolai Bukharin"), were accused, pleaded guilty of conspiracy against the Soviet Union, and were executed.[[138]](#cite_note-145)[[137]](#cite_note-World_War_II_2001-144)

The devastation of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") resulted in a massive recovery program involving the rebuilding of industrial plants, housing, and transportation as well as the demobilization and migration of millions of soldiers and civilians. In the midst of this turmoil during the winter of 1946–1947, the Soviet Union experienced the worst natural famine in the 20th century.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorlizki2004-146)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] There was no serious opposition to Stalin as the secret police continued to send possible suspects to the [gulag](/wiki/Gulag "Gulag"). Relations with the United States and Britain went from friendly to hostile, as they denounced Stalin's political controls over eastern Europe and his [Berlin Blockade](/wiki/Berlin_Blockade "Berlin Blockade"). By 1947, the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") had begun. Stalin himself believed that capitalism was a hollow shell and would crumble under increased non-military pressure exerted through proxies in countries like Italy. He greatly underestimated the economic strength of the West and instead of triumph saw the West build up alliances that were designed to permanently stop or contain Soviet expansion. In early 1950, Stalin gave the go-ahead for [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea")'s invasion of [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), expecting a short war. He was stunned when the Americans entered and defeated the North Koreans, putting them almost on the Soviet border. Stalin supported [China](/wiki/China "China")'s entry into the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War"), which drove the Americans back to the prewar boundaries, but which escalated tensions. The United States decided to mobilize its economy for a long contest with the Soviets, built the [hydrogen bomb](/wiki/Hydrogen_bomb "Hydrogen bomb"), and strengthened the [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") alliance that covered [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe").[[140]](#cite_note-147)

According to Gorlizki and Khlevniuk, Stalin's consistent and overriding goal after 1945 was to consolidate the nation's superpower status and in the face of his growing physical decrepitude, to maintain his own hold on total power. Stalin created a leadership system that reflected historic czarist styles of paternalism and repression yet was also quite modern. At the top, personal loyalty to Stalin counted for everything. Stalin also created powerful committees, elevated younger specialists, and began major institutional innovations. In the teeth of persecution, Stalin's deputies cultivated informal norms and mutual understandings which provided the foundations for collective rule after his death.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorlizki2004-146)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

For most Westerners and [anti-communist](/wiki/Anti-communist "Anti-communist") Russians, Stalin is viewed overwhelmingly negatively as a [mass murderer](/wiki/Mass_murder "Mass murder"); for significant numbers of Russians and Georgians, he is regarded as a great statesman and state-builder.[[141]](#cite_note-148)

#### China

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Mao_Proclaiming_New_China.JPG/250px-Mao_Proclaiming_New_China.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mao_Proclaiming_New_China.JPG)

[Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") proclaiming the foundation of the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/China "China") on October 1, 1949

After the [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War"), [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") and the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") came to power in 1949 as the [Nationalist government](/wiki/Nationalist_government "Nationalist government") headed by the [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") fled to the island of Taiwan. In 1950–1953, China engaged in a large-scale, undeclared war with the United States, South Korea, and United Nations forces in the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War"). While the war ended in a military stalemate, it gave Mao the opportunity to identify and purge elements in China that seemed supportive of capitalism. At first, there was close cooperation with Stalin, who sent in technical experts to aid the industrialization process along the line of the Soviet model of the 1930s.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown2009179–193-149) After Stalin's death in 1953, relations with Moscow soured – Mao thought Stalin's successors had betrayed the Communist ideal. Mao charged that Soviet leader [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev") was the leader of a "revisionist clique" which had turned against Marxism and Leninism and was now setting the stage for the restoration of capitalism.[[143]](#cite_note-150) The two nations were at sword's point by 1960. Both began forging alliances with communist supporters around the globe, thereby splitting the worldwide movement into two hostile camps.[[144]](#cite_note-151)

Rejecting the Soviet model of rapid urbanization, Mao Zedong and his top aide [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping") launched the [Great Leap Forward](/wiki/Great_Leap_Forward "Great Leap Forward") in 1957–1961 with the goal of industrializing China overnight, using the peasant villages as the base rather than large cities.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown2009316–332-152) Private ownership of land ended and the peasants worked in large collective farms that were now ordered to start up heavy industry operations, such as steel mills. Plants were built in remote locations, due to the lack of technical experts, managers, transportation, or needed facilities. Industrialization failed, and the main result was a sharp unexpected decline in agricultural output, which led to mass famine and millions of deaths. The years of the Great Leap Forward in fact saw economic regression, with 1958 through 1961 being the only years between 1953 and 1983 in which China's economy saw negative growth. Political economist [Dwight Perkins](/wiki/Dwight_H._Perkins_(economist) "Dwight H. Perkins (economist)") argues: "Enormous amounts of investment produced only modest increases in production or none at all. ... In short, the Great Leap was a very expensive disaster."[[146]](#cite_note-153) Put in charge of rescuing the economy, Deng adopted pragmatic policies that the idealistic Mao disliked. For a while, Mao was in the shadows but returned to center stage and purged Deng and his allies in the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution") (1966–1976).[[147]](#cite_note-154)

The [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution") was an upheaval that targeted intellectuals and party leaders from 1966 through 1976. Mao's goal was to purify communism by removing pro-capitalists and traditionalists by imposing [Maoist](/wiki/Maoist "Maoist") orthodoxy within the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party"). The movement paralyzed China politically and weakened the country economically, culturally, and intellectually for years. Millions of people were accused, humiliated, stripped of power, and either imprisoned, killed, or most often, sent to work as farm laborers. Mao insisted that those he labelled [revisionists](/wiki/Revisionism_(Marxism) "Revisionism (Marxism)") be removed through violent [class struggle](/wiki/Class_struggle "Class struggle"). The two most prominent militants were Marshall [Lin Biao](/wiki/Lin_Biao "Lin Biao") of the army and Mao's wife [Jiang Qing](/wiki/Jiang_Qing "Jiang Qing"). China's youth responded to Mao's appeal by forming [Red Guard](/wiki/Red_Guard "Red Guard") groups around the country. The movement spread into the military, urban workers, and the Communist party leadership itself. It resulted in widespread factional struggles in all walks of life. In the top leadership, it led to a mass purge of senior officials who were accused of taking a "[capitalist road](/wiki/Capitalist_road "Capitalist road")", most notably [Liu Shaoqi](/wiki/Liu_Shaoqi "Liu Shaoqi") and [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping"). During the same period, Mao's [personality cult](/wiki/Personality_cult "Personality cult") grew to immense proportions. After Mao's death in 1976, the survivors were rehabilitated and many returned to power.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown2009-155)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Mao's government was responsible for vast numbers of deaths with estimates ranging from 40 to 80 million victims through starvation, persecution, [prison labour](/wiki/Laogai "Laogai"), and mass executions.[[149]](#cite_note-156)[[150]](#cite_note-:6-157)[[151]](#cite_note-158)[[152]](#cite_note-159) Mao has also been praised for transforming China from a [semi-colony](/wiki/Semi-colony "Semi-colony") to a leading world power, with greatly advanced literacy, women's rights, basic healthcare, primary education, and life expectancy.[[153]](#cite_note-Bottelier-160)[[154]](#cite_note-161)[[155]](#cite_note-Galtung-162)[[156]](#cite_note-PopulationStudies2015-163)

### Cold War

Further information: [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") and [Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Communist_Block.svg/500px-Communist_Block.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Communist_Block.svg)

States that had communist governments

States that the Soviet Union believed at one point to be [moving toward socialism](/wiki/Moving_toward_socialism "Moving toward socialism")

Other states allied with the Soviet Union at some point

Its leading role in World War II saw the emergence of the [industrialized Soviet Union](/wiki/Industrialized_Soviet_Union "Industrialized Soviet Union") as a [superpower](/wiki/Superpower "Superpower").[[157]](#cite_note-Program_CPSS-164)[[158]](#cite_note-Nossal-165) Marxist–Leninist governments modeled on the Soviet Union took power with Soviet assistance in [Bulgaria](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_Bulgaria "People's Republic of Bulgaria"), [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovak_Socialist_Republic "Czechoslovak Socialist Republic"), [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany"), [Poland](/wiki/Polish_People%27s_Republic "Polish People's Republic"), Hungary, and [Romania](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Romania "Socialist Republic of Romania"). A Marxist–Leninist government was also created under [Josip Broz Tito](/wiki/Josip_Broz_Tito "Josip Broz Tito") in [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia"); Tito's independent policies led to the [Tito–Stalin split](/wiki/Tito%E2%80%93Stalin_split "Tito–Stalin split") and expulsion of Yugoslavia from the [Cominform](/wiki/Cominform "Cominform") in 1948, and [Titoism](/wiki/Titoism "Titoism") was branded *[deviationist](/wiki/Deviationist "Deviationist")*. [Albania](/wiki/People%27s_Socialist_Republic_of_Albania "People's Socialist Republic of Albania") also became an independent Marxist–Leninist state following the [Albanian–Soviet split](/wiki/Albanian%E2%80%93Soviet_split "Albanian–Soviet split") in 1960,[[132]](#cite_note-Bland_1995-139)[[133]](#cite_note-Bland_1997-140) resulting from an ideological fallout between [Enver Hoxha](/wiki/Enver_Hoxha "Enver Hoxha"), a Stalinist, and the Soviet government of [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev"), who enacted a period of [de-Stalinization](/wiki/De-Stalinization "De-Stalinization") and re-approached diplomatic relations with Yugoslavia in 1976.[[159]](#cite_note-166) The Communist Party of China, led by Mao Zedong, established the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China"), which would follow its own ideological path of development following the [Sino-Soviet split](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_split "Sino-Soviet split").[[160]](#cite_note-167) Communism was seen as a rival of and a threat to Western capitalism for most of the 20th century.[[161]](#cite_note-Georgakas1992-168)

In Western Europe, communist parties were part of several post-war governments, and even when the Cold War forced many of those countries to remove them from government, such as in Italy, they remained part of the [liberal-democratic](/wiki/Liberal-democratic "Liberal-democratic") process.[[162]](#cite_note-Kindersley-169)[[163]](#cite_note-170) There were also many developments in libertarian Marxism, especially during the 1960s with the [New Left](/wiki/New_Left "New Left").[[164]](#cite_note-171) By the 1960s and 1970s, many Western communist parties had criticized many of the actions of communist states, distanced from them, and developed a [democratic road to socialism](/wiki/Democratic_road_to_socialism "Democratic road to socialism"), which became known as [Eurocommunism](/wiki/Eurocommunism "Eurocommunism").[[162]](#cite_note-Kindersley-169) This development was criticized by more orthodox supporters of the Soviet Union as amounting to [social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy").[[165]](#cite_note-172)

Since 1957, [communists have been frequently voted into power](/wiki/Communism_in_Kerala "Communism in Kerala") in the [Indian](/wiki/India "India") state of [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala").[[166]](#cite_note-173)

In 1959, [Cuban revolutionaries](/wiki/Cuban_Revolution "Cuban Revolution") overthrew Cuba's previous government under the dictator [Fulgencio Batista](/wiki/Fulgencio_Batista "Fulgencio Batista").[[167]](#cite_note-174) While the revolutionaries were originally not uniformly communist, after their military victory, the new rebel officials underwent a sort of [radicalization alongside their political consolidation](/wiki/Consolidation_of_the_Cuban_Revolution "Consolidation of the Cuban Revolution"), that pushed the new government to eventually become Marxist-Leninist.[[168]](#cite_note-175)[[169]](#cite_note-176)

### Dissolution of the Soviet Union

Further information: [Dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union")

With the fall of the [Warsaw Pact](/wiki/Warsaw_Pact "Warsaw Pact") after the [Revolutions of 1989](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1989 "Revolutions of 1989"), which led to the fall of most of the former [Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc"), the Soviet Union was dissolved on 26 December 1991. It was a result of the declaration number 142-Н of the [Soviet of the Republics](/wiki/Soviet_of_the_Republics "Soviet of the Republics") of the [Supreme Soviet of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Supreme_Soviet_of_the_Soviet_Union "Supreme Soviet of the Soviet Union").[[170]](#cite_note-177) The declaration acknowledged the independence of the former [Soviet republics](/wiki/Soviet_republics "Soviet republics") and created the [Commonwealth of Independent States](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Independent_States "Commonwealth of Independent States"), although five of the signatories ratified it much later or did not do it at all. On the previous day, Soviet president [Mikhail Gorbachev](/wiki/Mikhail_Gorbachev "Mikhail Gorbachev") (the eighth and final [leader of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Soviet_Union "Leader of the Soviet Union")) resigned, declared his office extinct, and handed over its powers, including control of the [Cheget](/wiki/Cheget "Cheget"), to Russian president [Boris Yeltsin](/wiki/Boris_Yeltsin "Boris Yeltsin"). That evening at 7:32, the [Soviet flag](/wiki/Soviet_flag "Soviet flag") was lowered from the [Kremlin](/wiki/Kremlin "Kremlin") for the last time and replaced with the pre-revolutionary [Russian flag](/wiki/Russian_flag "Russian flag"). Previously, from August to December 1991, all the individual republics, including Russia itself, had seceded from the union. The week before the union's formal dissolution, eleven republics signed the [Alma-Ata Protocol](/wiki/Alma-Ata_Protocol "Alma-Ata Protocol"), formally establishing the [Commonwealth of Independent States](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Independent_States "Commonwealth of Independent States"), and declared that the Soviet Union had ceased to exist.[[171]](#cite_note-178)[[172]](#cite_note-179)

### Post-Soviet communism

See also: [List of socialist parties with national parliamentary representation](/wiki/List_of_socialist_parties_with_national_parliamentary_representation "List of socialist parties with national parliamentary representation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/18th_National_Congress_of_the_Communist_Party_of_China.jpg/250px-18th_National_Congress_of_the_Communist_Party_of_China.jpg)](/wiki/File:18th_National_Congress_of_the_Communist_Party_of_China.jpg)

[18th National Congress](/wiki/18th_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "18th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party") of the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Communist_flag_at_night_at_Ho_Chi_Minh_City%2C_Vietnam%2C_year_2024.jpg/250px-Communist_flag_at_night_at_Ho_Chi_Minh_City%2C_Vietnam%2C_year_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Communist_flag_at_night_at_Ho_Chi_Minh_City,_Vietnam,_year_2024.jpg)

[Communist](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Vietnam "Communist Party of Vietnam") flag at night at Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam, 2024

As of 2023, states controlled by Communist parties under a single-party system include the People's Republic of China, the [Republic of Cuba](/wiki/Republic_of_Cuba "Republic of Cuba"), the [Democratic People's Republic of Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea"), the [Lao People's Democratic Republic](/wiki/Lao_People%27s_Democratic_Republic "Lao People's Democratic Republic"), and the [Socialist Republic of Vietnam](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Vietnam "Socialist Republic of Vietnam"). Communist parties, or their descendant parties, remain politically important in several other countries. With the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union") and the [Fall of Communism](/wiki/Fall_of_Communism "Fall of Communism"), there was a split between those hardline Communists, sometimes referred to in the media as *[neo-Stalinists](/wiki/Neo-Stalinist "Neo-Stalinist")*, who remained committed to orthodox Marxism–Leninism, and those, such as [The Left](/wiki/The_Left_(Germany) "The Left (Germany)") in Germany, who work within the liberal-democratic process for a democratic road to socialism;[[173]](#cite_note-180) other ruling Communist parties became closer to [democratic socialist](/wiki/Democratic_socialist "Democratic socialist") and [social democratic](/wiki/Social_democratic "Social democratic") parties.[[67]](#cite_note-Peter-73) Outside Communist states, reformed Communist parties have led or been part of left-leaning government or regional coalitions, including in the former Eastern Bloc. In Nepal, Communists ([CPN UML](/wiki/CPN_UML "CPN UML") and [Nepal Communist Party](/wiki/Nepal_Communist_Party "Nepal Communist Party")) were part of the [1st Nepalese Constituent Assembly](/wiki/1st_Nepalese_Constituent_Assembly "1st Nepalese Constituent Assembly"), which abolished the monarchy in 2008 and turned the country into a federal liberal-democratic republic, and have democratically shared power with other communists, Marxist–Leninists, and [Maoists](/wiki/Maoists "Maoists") ([CPN Maoist](/wiki/CPN_Maoist "CPN Maoist")), social democrats ([Nepali Congress](/wiki/Nepali_Congress "Nepali Congress")), and others as part of their [People's Multiparty Democracy](/wiki/People%27s_Multiparty_Democracy "People's Multiparty Democracy").[[68]](#cite_note-The_Economist-74)[[69]](#cite_note-The_Diplomat-75) The [Communist Party of the Russian Federation](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Russian_Federation "Communist Party of the Russian Federation") has some supporters, but is reformist rather than revolutionary, aiming to lessen the inequalities of Russia's market economy.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallDagger2019-1)

In China, the [reform and opening up](/wiki/Reform_and_opening_up "Reform and opening up") was started in 1978 under the leadership of [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping"), and since then China has managed to bring down the poverty rate from 53% in the Mao era to just 8% in 2001.[[174]](#cite_note-181) After losing Soviet subsidies and support, Vietnam and Cuba have attracted more foreign investment to their countries, with their economies becoming more market-oriented.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallDagger2019-1) North Korea, the last Communist country that still practices Soviet-style Communism, is both repressive and isolationist.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallDagger2019-1)

## Theory

Communist political thought and theory are diverse but share several core elements.[[a]](#cite_note-182) The dominant forms of communism are based on [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism") or [Leninism](/wiki/Leninism "Leninism") but non-Marxist versions of communism also exist, such as [anarcho-communism](/wiki/Anarcho-communism "Anarcho-communism") and [Christian communism](/wiki/Christian_communism "Christian communism"), which remain partly influenced by Marxist theories, such as [libertarian Marxism](/wiki/Libertarian_Marxism "Libertarian Marxism") and [humanist Marxism](/wiki/Humanist_Marxism "Humanist Marxism") in particular. Common elements include being theoretical rather than ideological, identifying political parties not by ideology but by class and economic interest, and identifying with the proletariat. According to communists, the proletariat can avoid mass unemployment only if capitalism is overthrown; in the short run, state-oriented communists favor [state ownership](/wiki/State_ownership "State ownership") of the [commanding heights of the economy](/wiki/Commanding_heights_of_the_economy "Commanding heights of the economy") as a means to defend the proletariat from capitalist pressure. Some communists are distinguished by other Marxists in seeing peasants and smallholders of property as possible allies in their goal of shortening the abolition of capitalism.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanson2001-183)

For Leninist communism, such goals, including short-term proletarian interests to improve their political and material conditions, can only be achieved through [vanguardism](/wiki/Vanguardism "Vanguardism"), an elitist form of [socialism from above](/wiki/Socialism_from_above "Socialism from above") that relies on theoretical analysis to identify proletarian interests rather than consulting the proletarians themselves,[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanson2001-183) as is advocated by [libertarian](/wiki/Libertarian "Libertarian") communists.[[10]](#cite_note-Kinna_2012-10) When they engage in elections, Leninist communists' main task is that of educating voters in what are deemed their true interests rather than in response to the expression of interest by voters themselves. When they have gained control of the state, Leninist communists' main task was preventing other political parties from deceiving the proletariat, such as by running their own independent candidates. This vanguardist approach comes from their commitments to [democratic centralism](/wiki/Democratic_centralism "Democratic centralism") in which communists can only be cadres, i.e. members of the party who are full-time professional revolutionaries, as was conceived by [Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin").[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanson2001-183)

### Marxist communism

Main article: [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism")

See also: [List of communist ideologies](/wiki/List_of_communist_ideologies "List of communist ideologies") and [Marxist schools of thought](/wiki/Marxist_schools_of_thought "Marxist schools of thought")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Marx_et_Engels_%C3%A0_Shanghai.jpg/250px-Marx_et_Engels_%C3%A0_Shanghai.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marx_et_Engels_%C3%A0_Shanghai.jpg)

A monument dedicated to [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") (left) and [Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels") (right) in Shanghai

Marxism is a method of [socioeconomic](/wiki/Socioeconomic "Socioeconomic") analysis that uses a [materialist](/wiki/Materialist "Materialist") interpretation of historical development, better known as [historical materialism](/wiki/Historical_materialism "Historical materialism"), to understand [social class](/wiki/Social_class "Social class") relations and [social conflict](/wiki/Social_conflict "Social conflict") and a [dialectical](/wiki/Dialectic "Dialectic") perspective to view [social transformation](/wiki/Social_transformation "Social transformation"). It originates from the works of 19th-century German philosophers [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") and [Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels"). As Marxism has developed over time into various branches and [schools of thought](/wiki/Schools_of_thought "Schools of thought"), no single, definitive [Marxist theory](/wiki/Marxist_theory "Marxist theory") exists.[[176]](#cite_note-Wolff_and_Resnick,_1987-184) Marxism considers itself to be the embodiment of [scientific socialism](/wiki/Scientific_socialism "Scientific socialism") but does not model an ideal society based on the design of [intellectuals](/wiki/Intellectual "Intellectual"), whereby communism is seen as a [state of affairs](/wiki/State_of_affairs_(sociology) "State of affairs (sociology)") to be established based on any intelligent design; rather, it is a non-[idealist](/wiki/Idealist "Idealist") attempt at the understanding of material history and society, whereby communism is the expression of a real movement, with parameters that are derived from actual life.[[177]](#cite_note-185)

According to Marxist theory, class conflict arises in capitalist societies due to contradictions between the material interests of the oppressed and exploited [proletariat](/wiki/Proletariat "Proletariat") – a class of wage laborers employed to produce goods and services – and the [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie") – the [ruling class](/wiki/Ruling_class "Ruling class") that owns the [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production") and extracts its wealth through appropriation of the [surplus product](/wiki/Surplus_product "Surplus product") produced by the proletariat in the form of [profit](/wiki/Profit_(economics) "Profit (economics)"). This class struggle that is commonly expressed as the revolt of a society's [productive forces](/wiki/Productive_forces "Productive forces") against its [relations of production](/wiki/Relations_of_production "Relations of production"), results in a period of short-term crises as the bourgeoisie struggle to manage the intensifying [alienation of labor](/wiki/Alienation_of_labor "Alienation of labor") experienced by the proletariat, albeit with varying degrees of [class consciousness](/wiki/Class_consciousness "Class consciousness"). In periods of deep crisis, the resistance of the oppressed can culminate in a [proletarian revolution](/wiki/Proletarian_revolution "Proletarian revolution") which, if victorious, leads to the establishment of the [socialist mode of production](/wiki/Socialist_mode_of_production "Socialist mode of production") based on [social ownership](/wiki/Social_ownership "Social ownership") of the means of production, "[To each according to his contribution](/wiki/To_each_according_to_his_contribution "To each according to his contribution")", and [production for use](/wiki/Production_for_use "Production for use"). As the productive forces continued to advance, the [communist society](/wiki/Communist_society "Communist society"), i.e. a classless, stateless, humane society based on [common ownership](/wiki/Common_ownership "Common ownership"), follows the maxim "[From each according to his ability, to each according to his needs](/wiki/From_each_according_to_his_ability,_to_each_according_to_his_needs "From each according to his ability, to each according to his needs")."[[82]](#cite_note-Hudis_et_al._2018-88)

While it originates from the works of Marx and Engels, Marxism has developed into many different branches and schools of thought, with the result that there is now no single definitive Marxist theory.[[176]](#cite_note-Wolff_and_Resnick,_1987-184) Different Marxian schools place a greater emphasis on certain aspects of [classical Marxism](/wiki/Classical_Marxism "Classical Marxism") while rejecting or modifying other aspects. Many schools of thought have sought to combine Marxian concepts and non-Marxian concepts, which has then led to contradictory conclusions.[[178]](#cite_note-186) There is a movement toward the recognition that historical materialism and [dialectical materialism](/wiki/Dialectical_materialism "Dialectical materialism") remain the fundamental aspects of all [Marxist schools of thought](/wiki/Marxist_schools_of_thought "Marxist schools of thought").[[94]](#cite_note-Ermak_2019-100) Marxism–Leninism and its offshoots are the most well-known of these and have been a driving force in [international relations](/wiki/International_relations "International relations") during most of the 20th century.[[179]](#cite_note-Columbia-187)

Classical Marxism is the economic, philosophical, and sociological theories expounded by Marx and Engels as contrasted with later developments in Marxism, especially Leninism and Marxism–Leninism.[[180]](#cite_note-188) [Orthodox Marxism](/wiki/Orthodox_Marxism "Orthodox Marxism") is the body of Marxist thought that emerged after the death of Marx and which became the official philosophy of the socialist movement as represented in the [Second International](/wiki/Second_International "Second International") until World War I in 1914. Orthodox Marxism aims to simplify, codify, and systematize Marxist method and theory by clarifying the perceived ambiguities and contradictions of classical Marxism. The philosophy of orthodox Marxism includes the understanding that material development (advances in technology in the [productive forces](/wiki/Productive_forces "Productive forces")) is the primary agent of change in the structure of society and of human social relations and that social systems and their relations (e.g. [feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism"), [capitalism](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism"), and so on) become contradictory and inefficient as the productive forces develop, which results in some form of social revolution arising in response to the mounting contradictions. This revolutionary change is the vehicle for fundamental society-wide changes and ultimately leads to the emergence of new [economic systems](/wiki/Economic_system "Economic system").[[181]](#cite_note-189) As a term, *orthodox Marxism* represents the methods of historical materialism and of dialectical materialism, and not the normative aspects inherent to classical Marxism, without implying dogmatic adherence to the results of Marx's investigations.[[182]](#cite_note-190)

#### Marxist concepts

##### Class conflict and historical materialism

Main articles: [Class conflict](/wiki/Class_conflict "Class conflict") and [Historical materialism](/wiki/Historical_materialism "Historical materialism")

At the root of Marxism is historical materialism, the [materialist](/wiki/Materialist "Materialist") conception of history which holds that the key characteristic of economic systems through history has been the [mode of production](/wiki/Mode_of_production "Mode of production") and that the change between modes of production has been triggered by class struggle. According to this analysis, the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") ushered the world into the new [capitalist mode of production](/wiki/Capitalist_mode_of_production_(Marxist_theory) "Capitalist mode of production (Marxist theory)"). Before capitalism, certain [working classes](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") had ownership of instruments used in production; however, because machinery was much more efficient, this property became worthless and the mass majority of workers could only survive by selling their labor to make use of someone else's machinery, and making someone else profit. Accordingly, capitalism divided the world between two major classes, namely that of the [proletariat](/wiki/Proletariat "Proletariat") and the [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie"). These classes are directly antagonistic as the latter possesses [private ownership](/wiki/Private_ownership "Private ownership") of the [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production"), earning profit via the [surplus value](/wiki/Surplus_value "Surplus value") generated by the proletariat, who have no ownership of the means of production and therefore no option but to sell its labor to the bourgeoisie.[[183]](#cite_note-191)

According to the materialist conception of history, it is through the furtherance of its own material interests that the rising bourgeoisie within [feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism") captured power and abolished, of all relations of private property, only the feudal privilege, thereby taking the feudal [ruling class](/wiki/Ruling_class "Ruling class") out of existence. This was another key element behind the consolidation of capitalism as the new mode of production, the final expression of class and property relations that has led to a massive expansion of production.
