It is only in capitalism that private property in itself can be abolished.[[184]](#cite_note-192) Similarly, the proletariat would capture political power, abolish bourgeois property through the [common ownership](/wiki/Common_ownership "Common ownership") of the means of production, therefore abolishing the bourgeoisie, ultimately abolishing the proletariat itself and ushering the world into [communism as a new mode of production](/wiki/Communist_society "Communist society"). In between capitalism and communism, there is the [dictatorship of the proletariat](/wiki/Dictatorship_of_the_proletariat "Dictatorship of the proletariat"); it is the defeat of the [bourgeois state](/wiki/Bourgeois_state "Bourgeois state") but not yet of the capitalist mode of production, and at the same time the only element which places into the realm of possibility moving on from this mode of production. This *dictatorship*, based on the [Paris Commune](/wiki/Paris_Commune "Paris Commune")'s model,[[185]](#cite_note-193) is to be the most democratic state where the whole of the public authority is elected and recallable under the basis of [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage").[[186]](#cite_note-194)

##### Critique of political economy

Main article: [Critique of political economy](/wiki/Critique_of_political_economy "Critique of political economy")

Critique of [political economy](/wiki/Political_economy "Political economy") is a form of [social critique](/wiki/Social_critique "Social critique") that rejects the various social categories and structures that constitute the mainstream discourse concerning the forms and modalities of resource allocation and income distribution in the economy. Communists, such as Marx and Engels, are described as prominent critics of political economy.[[187]](#cite_note-195)[[188]](#cite_note-196)[[189]](#cite_note-197) The critique rejects economists' use of what its advocates believe are unrealistic [axioms](/wiki/Axiom "Axiom"), faulty historical assumptions, and the normative use of various descriptive narratives.[[190]](#cite_note-198) They reject what they describe as mainstream economists' tendency to posit the economy as an *[a priori](/wiki/A_priori "A priori")* societal category.[[191]](#cite_note-Reading_Capital-199) Those who engage in critique of economy tend to reject the view that the economy and its categories is to be understood as something [transhistorical](/wiki/Transhistorical "Transhistorical").[[192]](#cite_note-200)[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPostone199544,_192–216-201) It is seen as merely one of many types of historically specific ways to distribute resources. They argue that it is a relatively new mode of resource distribution, which emerged along with modernity.[[194]](#cite_note-202)[[195]](#cite_note-203)[[196]](#cite_note-204)

Critics of economy critique the given status of the economy itself, and do not aim to create theories regarding how to administer economies.[[197]](#cite_note-205)[[198]](#cite_note-206) Critics of economy commonly view what is most commonly referred to as the economy as being bundles of [metaphysical](/wiki/Metaphysical "Metaphysical") concepts, as well as societal and normative practices, rather than being the result of any self-evident or proclaimed economic laws.[[191]](#cite_note-Reading_Capital-199) They also tend to consider the views which are commonplace within the field of economics as faulty, or simply as [pseudoscience](/wiki/Pseudoscience "Pseudoscience").[[199]](#cite_note-207)[[200]](#cite_note-208) Into the 21st century, there are multiple critiques of political economy; what they have in common is the critique of what critics of political economy tend to view as [dogma](/wiki/Dogma "Dogma"), i.e. claims of the economy as a necessary and transhistorical societal category.[[201]](#cite_note-209)

##### Marxian economics

Main article: [Marxian economics](/wiki/Marxian_economics "Marxian economics")

Marxian economics and its proponents view capitalism as economically unsustainable and incapable of improving the living standards of the population due to its need to compensate for [falling rates of profit](/wiki/Falling_rates_of_profit "Falling rates of profit") by cutting employee's wages, social benefits, and pursuing military aggression. The [communist mode of production](/wiki/Communist_mode_of_production "Communist mode of production") would succeed capitalism as humanity's new mode of production through workers' [revolution](/wiki/Revolution "Revolution"). According to Marxian [crisis theory](/wiki/Crisis_theory "Crisis theory"), communism is not an inevitability but an economic necessity.[[202]](#cite_note-210)

##### Socialization versus nationalization

Main articles: [Socialization (economics)](/wiki/Socialization_(economics) "Socialization (economics)") and [Socialization (Marxism)](/wiki/Socialization_(Marxism) "Socialization (Marxism)")

An important concept in Marxism is socialization, i.e. [social ownership](/wiki/Social_ownership "Social ownership"), versus [nationalization](/wiki/Nationalization "Nationalization"). Nationalization is [state ownership](/wiki/State_ownership "State ownership") of property whereas socialization is control and management of property by society. Marxism considers the latter as its goal and considers nationalization a tactical issue, as state ownership is still in the realm of the [capitalist mode of production](/wiki/Capitalist_mode_of_production_(Marxist_theory) "Capitalist mode of production (Marxist theory)"). In the words of Friedrich Engels, "the transformation ... into State-ownership does not do away with the capitalistic nature of the productive forces. ... State-ownership of the productive forces is not the solution of the conflict, but concealed within it are the technical conditions that form the elements of that solution."[[b]](#cite_note-:0-211) This has led Marxist groups and tendencies critical of the [Soviet model](/wiki/Soviet_model "Soviet model") to label states based on nationalization, such as the Soviet Union, as [state capitalist](/wiki/State_capitalist "State capitalist"), a view that is also shared by several scholars.[[45]](#cite_note-Chomsky,_Howard,_Fitzgibbons-49)[[129]](#cite_note-The_Soviet_Union_Has_an_Administered,_Not_a_Planned,_Economy,_1985-136)[[131]](#cite_note-Ellman_2007-138)

##### Democracy in Marxism

These paragraphs are an excerpt from [Democracy in Marxism](/wiki/Democracy_in_Marxism "Democracy in Marxism").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Democracy_in_Marxism&action=edit)]

[Marxist theory](/wiki/Marxist_theory "Marxist theory") envisions that a new democratic society would rise through the organized actions of the international [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class"), enfranchising the entire population and freeing up humans to act without being bound by the [labour market](/wiki/Labour_market "Labour market").[[203]](#cite_note-Democracy_in_Marxism_Calhoun2002-23-212)[[204]](#cite_note-Democracy_in_Marxism_Clark1998-213) There would be little, if any, need for a [state](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)"), the goal of which was to enforce the [alienation of labour](/wiki/Alienation_of_labour "Alienation of labour");[[203]](#cite_note-Democracy_in_Marxism_Calhoun2002-23-212) as such, the state would eventually [wither away](/wiki/Withering_away_of_the_state "Withering away of the state") as its conditions of existence disappear.[[205]](#cite_note-214)[[206]](#cite_note-215)[[207]](#cite_note-216)

[Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") and [Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels") stated in *[The Communist Manifesto](/wiki/The_Communist_Manifesto "The Communist Manifesto")* (1848) and later works that "the first step in the revolution by the working class, is to raise the [proletariat](/wiki/Proletariat "Proletariat") to the position of ruling class, to win the battle of democracy", and [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage") being "one of the first and most important tasks of the militant proletariat".[[208]](#cite_note-217)[[209]](#cite_note-218)[[210]](#cite_note-219) As Marx wrote in his *[Critique of the Gotha Programme](/wiki/Critique_of_the_Gotha_Programme "Critique of the Gotha Programme")* (1875), "between [capitalist](/wiki/Capitalist "Capitalist") and [communist society](/wiki/Communist_society "Communist society") there lies the period of the revolutionary transformation of the one into the other. Corresponding to this is also a political transition period in which the state can be nothing but the revolutionary [dictatorship of the proletariat](/wiki/Dictatorship_of_the_proletariat "Dictatorship of the proletariat")".[[211]](#cite_note-Democracy_in_Marxism_Karl_Marx:Critique_of_the_Gotha_Programme-220) He allowed for the possibility of [peaceful transition](/wiki/Peaceful_transition_of_power "Peaceful transition of power") in some countries with strong democratic institutional structures (Britain, the United States, and the Netherlands) but suggested that in other countries in which workers can not "attain their goal by peaceful means", the "lever of our revolution must be force" on the grounds that the working people had the [right to revolt](/wiki/Right_to_revolt "Right to revolt") if they were denied political expression.[[212]](#cite_note-221)[[213]](#cite_note-222)

In response to the question "What will be the course of this revolution?" in *[The Principles of Communism](/wiki/The_Principles_of_Communism "The Principles of Communism")* (1847), [Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels") wrote: "Above all, it will establish a democratic constitution, and through this, the direct or indirect dominance of the proletariat."[[214]](#cite_note-223) While [Marxists](/wiki/Marxists "Marxists") propose replacing the [bourgeois state](/wiki/Bourgeois_state "Bourgeois state") with a proletarian semi-state through revolution ([dictatorship of the proletariat](/wiki/Dictatorship_of_the_proletariat "Dictatorship of the proletariat")), which would eventually wither away, [anarchists](/wiki/Anarchists "Anarchists") warn that the state must be abolished along with capitalism. Nonetheless, the desired end results (a stateless [communal society](/wiki/Communal_society "Communal society")) are the same.[[215]](#cite_note-224)

Marx criticized [liberalism](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism") as not democratic enough and found the unequal social situation of the workers during the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") undermined the democratic agency of citizens.[[216]](#cite_note-225) Some argue democratic decision-making consistent with Marxism should include voting on how [surplus labor](/wiki/Surplus_labor "Surplus labor") is to be organized.[[217]](#cite_note-226)

Marxists differ in their positions towards democracy;[[218]](#cite_note-227)[[219]](#cite_note-228) in the words of Robert Meister, "controversy over Marx's legacy today turns largely on its ambiguous relation to democracy."[[220]](#cite_note-229) The stance on [political violence](/wiki/Political_violence "Political violence") varies among Marxists, while Marx saw peaceful means possible,[[221]](#cite_note-230) Lenin affirmed political violence and [political terror](/wiki/Terror_(politics) "Terror (politics)").[[222]](#cite_note-231)

### Leninist communism

Main article: [Leninism](/wiki/Leninism "Leninism")

> We want to achieve a new and better order of society: in this new and better society there must be neither rich nor poor; all will have to work. Not a handful of rich people, but all the working people must enjoy the fruits of their common labour. Machines and other improvements must serve to ease the work of all and not to enable a few to grow rich at the expense of millions and tens of millions of people. This new and better society is called socialist society. The teachings about this society are called "socialism".

Vladimir Lenin, *To the Rural Poor* (1903)[[223]](#cite_note-232)

Leninism is a political ideology developed by Russian [Marxist](/wiki/Marxist "Marxist") revolutionary [Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin") that proposes the establishment of the [dictatorship of the proletariat](/wiki/Dictatorship_of_the_proletariat#Vladimir_Lenin "Dictatorship of the proletariat"), led by a revolutionary [vanguard party](/wiki/Vanguard_party "Vanguard party"), as the political prelude to the establishment of communism. The function of the Leninist vanguard party is to provide the working classes with the [political consciousness](/wiki/Political_consciousness "Political consciousness") (education and organisation) and revolutionary leadership necessary to depose [capitalism](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism") in the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") (1721–1917).[[224]](#cite_note-Modern_Thought_Third_Edition_1999_pp._476-233)

Leninist revolutionary leadership is based upon *[The Communist Manifesto](/wiki/The_Communist_Manifesto "The Communist Manifesto")* (1848), identifying the [Communist party](/wiki/Communist_party "Communist party") as "the most advanced and resolute section of the working class parties of every country; that section which pushes forward all others." As the vanguard party, the [Bolsheviks](/wiki/Bolsheviks "Bolsheviks") viewed history through the theoretical framework of [dialectical materialism](/wiki/Dialectical_materialism "Dialectical materialism"), which sanctioned political commitment to the successful overthrow of capitalism, and then to instituting [socialism](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism"); and as the revolutionary national government, to realize the socio-economic transition by all means.[[225]](#cite_note-Leninism,_p._265-234)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

#### Marxism–Leninism

Main article: [Marxism–Leninism](/wiki/Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism "Marxism–Leninism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Lenin-statue-in-Kolkata.jpg/250px-Lenin-statue-in-Kolkata.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lenin-statue-in-Kolkata.jpg)

[Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin") statue in Kolkata, West Bengal, India

[Marxism–Leninism](/wiki/Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism "Marxism–Leninism") is a political ideology developed by [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin").[[226]](#cite_note-made_by_stalin-235) According to its proponents, it is based on Marxism and Leninism. It describes the specific political ideology which Stalin implemented in the [Communist Party of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Communist Party of the Soviet Union") and in a global scale in the [Comintern](/wiki/Comintern "Comintern"). There is no definite agreement between historians about whether Stalin actually followed the principles of Marx and Lenin.[[227]](#cite_note-stalin_follow_marx_lenin-236) It also contains aspects which according to some are deviations from Marxism such as [socialism in one country](/wiki/Socialism_in_one_country "Socialism in one country").[[228]](#cite_note-sioc1-237)[[229]](#cite_note-sioc2-238) Marxism–Leninism was the official ideology of 20th-century [Communist parties](/wiki/Communist_parties "Communist parties") (including [Trotskyist](/wiki/Trotskyist "Trotskyist")), and was developed after the death of Lenin; its three principles were [dialectical materialism](/wiki/Dialectical_materialism "Dialectical materialism"), the [leading role of the Communist party](/wiki/Leading_role_of_the_Communist_party "Leading role of the Communist party") through [democratic centralism](/wiki/Democratic_centralism "Democratic centralism"), and a [planned economy](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy") with [industrialization](/wiki/Industrialization "Industrialization") and [agricultural collectivization](/wiki/Agricultural_collectivization "Agricultural collectivization"). *Marxism–Leninism* is misleading because Marx and Lenin never sanctioned or supported the creation of an *-ism* after them, and is revealing because, being popularized after Lenin's death by Stalin, it contained those three doctrinal and institutionalized principles that became a model for later Soviet-type regimes; its global influence, having at its height covered at least one-third of the world's population, has made *Marxist–Leninist* a convenient label for the [Communist bloc](/wiki/Communist_bloc "Communist bloc") as a dynamic ideological order.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanson20019298–9302Morgan2015-239)[[c]](#cite_note-240)

During the Cold War, Marxism–Leninism was the ideology of the most clearly visible communist movement and is the most prominent ideology associated with communism.[[179]](#cite_note-Columbia-187)[[note 8]](#cite_note-243) [Social fascism](/wiki/Social_fascism "Social fascism") was a theory supported by the Comintern and affiliated [Communist parties](/wiki/Communist_parties "Communist parties") during the early 1930s, which held that [social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy") was a variant of [fascism](/wiki/Fascism "Fascism") because it stood in the way of a [dictatorship of the proletariat](/wiki/Dictatorship_of_the_proletariat "Dictatorship of the proletariat"), in addition to a supposedly shared [corporatist](/wiki/Corporatist "Corporatist") economic model.[[232]](#cite_note-Haro_2011-244) At the time, leaders of the Comintern, such as Stalin and [Rajani Palme Dutt](/wiki/Rajani_Palme_Dutt "Rajani Palme Dutt"), stated that [capitalist](/wiki/Capitalist "Capitalist") society had entered the [Third Period](/wiki/Third_Period "Third Period") in which a [proletariat revolution](/wiki/Proletariat_revolution "Proletariat revolution") was imminent but could be prevented by social democrats and other *fascist* forces.[[232]](#cite_note-Haro_2011-244)[[233]](#cite_note-Hoppe_2011-245) The term *social fascist* was used pejoratively to describe social-democratic parties, anti-Comintern and progressive socialist parties and dissenters within Comintern affiliates throughout the [interwar period](/wiki/Interwar_period "Interwar period"). The social fascism theory was advocated vociferously by the [Communist Party of Germany](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Germany "Communist Party of Germany"), which was largely controlled and funded by the Soviet leadership from 1928.[[233]](#cite_note-Hoppe_2011-245)

Within Marxism–Leninism, [anti-revisionism](/wiki/Anti-revisionism "Anti-revisionism") is a position which emerged in the 1950s in opposition to the reforms and [Khrushchev Thaw](/wiki/Khrushchev_Thaw "Khrushchev Thaw") of Soviet leader [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev"). Where Khrushchev pursued an interpretation that differed from Stalin, the anti-revisionists within the international communist movement remained dedicated to Stalin's ideological legacy and criticized the Soviet Union under Khrushchev and his successors as [state capitalist](/wiki/State_capitalist "State capitalist") and [social imperialist](/wiki/Social_imperialist "Social imperialist") due to its hopes of achieving peace with the United States. The term *Stalinism* is also used to describe these positions but is often not used by its supporters who opine that Stalin practiced [orthodox Marxism](/wiki/Orthodox_Marxism "Orthodox Marxism") and Leninism. Because different political trends trace the historical roots of revisionism to different eras and leaders, there is significant disagreement today as to what constitutes anti-revisionism. Modern groups which describe themselves as anti-revisionist fall into several categories. Some uphold the works of Stalin and Mao Zedong and some the works of Stalin while rejecting Mao and universally tend to oppose [Trotskyism](/wiki/Trotskyism "Trotskyism"). Others reject both Stalin and Mao, tracing their ideological roots back to Marx and Lenin. In addition, other groups uphold various less-well-known historical leaders such as [Enver Hoxha](/wiki/Enver_Hoxha "Enver Hoxha"), who also broke with Mao during the [Sino-Albanian split](/wiki/Sino-Albanian_split "Sino-Albanian split").[[132]](#cite_note-Bland_1995-139)[[133]](#cite_note-Bland_1997-140) *Social imperialism* was a term used by Mao to criticize the Soviet Union post-Stalin. Mao stated that the Soviet Union had itself become an [imperialist](/wiki/Imperialist "Imperialist") power while maintaining a socialist *façade*.[[234]](#cite_note-246) Hoxha agreed with Mao in this analysis, before later using the expression to also condemn Mao's [Three Worlds Theory](/wiki/Three_Worlds_Theory "Three Worlds Theory").[[235]](#cite_note-247)

##### Stalinism

Main article: [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Joseph_Stalin_in_1932_%284%29_%28cropped%29%282%29.jpg/250px-Joseph_Stalin_in_1932_%284%29_%28cropped%29%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joseph_Stalin_in_1932_(4)_(cropped)(2).jpg)

[Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin"), the longest-serving [leader of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Soviet_Union "Leader of the Soviet Union")

Stalinism represents Stalin's style of governance as opposed to Marxism–Leninism, the [socioeconomic system](/wiki/Socioeconomic_system "Socioeconomic system") and [political ideology](/wiki/Political_ideology "Political ideology") implemented by Stalin in the Soviet Union, and later adapted by other states based on the [ideological Soviet model](/wiki/Ideological_Soviet_model "Ideological Soviet model"), such as [central planning](/wiki/Central_planning "Central planning"), [nationalization](/wiki/Nationalization "Nationalization"), and [one-party state](/wiki/One-party_state "One-party state"), along with [public ownership](/wiki/Public_ownership "Public ownership") of the [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production"), accelerated [industrialization](/wiki/Industrialization "Industrialization"), pro-active development of society's [productive forces](/wiki/Productive_forces "Productive forces") (research and development), and nationalized [natural resources](/wiki/Natural_resources "Natural resources"). Marxism–Leninism remained after [de-Stalinization](/wiki/De-Stalinization "De-Stalinization") whereas Stalinism did not. In the last letters before his death, Lenin warned against the danger of Stalin's personality and urged the Soviet government to replace him.[[94]](#cite_note-Ermak_2019-100) Until the [death of Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Death_of_Joseph_Stalin "Death of Joseph Stalin") in 1953, the Soviet Communist party referred to its own ideology as *Marxism–Leninism–Stalinism*.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanson2001-183)

Marxism–Leninism has been criticized by other communist and Marxist tendencies, which state that Marxist–Leninist states did not establish socialism but rather [state capitalism](/wiki/State_capitalism "State capitalism").[[45]](#cite_note-Chomsky,_Howard,_Fitzgibbons-49)[[129]](#cite_note-The_Soviet_Union_Has_an_Administered,_Not_a_Planned,_Economy,_1985-136)[[131]](#cite_note-Ellman_2007-138) According to Marxism, the dictatorship of the proletariat represents the rule of the majority (democracy) rather than of one party, to the extent that the co-founder of Marxism, Friedrich Engels, described its "specific form" as the [democratic republic](/wiki/Republicanism "Republicanism").[[236]](#cite_note-248) According to Engels, state property by itself is private property of capitalist nature,[[b]](#cite_note-:0-211) unless the proletariat has control of political power, in which case it forms public property.[[e]](#cite_note-249) Whether the proletariat was actually in control of the Marxist–Leninist states is a matter of debate between Marxism–Leninism and other communist tendencies. To these tendencies, Marxism–Leninism is neither Marxism nor Leninism nor the union of both but rather an artificial term created to justify Stalin's ideological distortion,[[237]](#cite_note-stalin_distortion-250) forced into the Communist Party of the Soviet Union and the Comintern. In the Soviet Union, this struggle against Marxism–Leninism was represented by [Trotskyism](/wiki/Trotskyism "Trotskyism"), which describes itself as a Marxist and Leninist tendency.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanson2001-183)

##### Trotskyism

Main article: [Trotskyism](/wiki/Trotskyism "Trotskyism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Mexico_-_Bellas_Artes_-_Fresque_Riviera_%C2%AB_Man_at_the_Crossroads_%C2%BB.JPG/250px-Mexico_-_Bellas_Artes_-_Fresque_Riviera_%C2%AB_Man_at_the_Crossroads_%C2%BB.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mexico_-_Bellas_Artes_-_Fresque_Riviera_%C2%AB_Man_at_the_Crossroads_%C2%BB.JPG)

Detail of *Man, Controller of the Universe*, fresco at [Palacio de Bellas Artes](/wiki/Palacio_de_Bellas_Artes "Palacio de Bellas Artes") in Mexico City showing [Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky"), [Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels"), and [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx")

Trotskyism, developed by [Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky") in opposition to [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism"),[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatenaude2017199-251) is a Marxist and Leninist tendency that supports the theory of [permanent revolution](/wiki/Permanent_revolution "Permanent revolution") and [world revolution](/wiki/World_revolution "World revolution") rather than the [two-stage theory](/wiki/Two-stage_theory "Two-stage theory") and Stalin's [socialism in one country](/wiki/Socialism_in_one_country "Socialism in one country"). It supported another communist revolution in the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") and [proletarian internationalism](/wiki/Proletarian_internationalism "Proletarian internationalism").[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatenaude2017193-252)

Rather than representing the [dictatorship of the proletariat](/wiki/Dictatorship_of_the_proletariat "Dictatorship of the proletariat"), Trotsky claimed that the Soviet Union had become a [degenerated workers' state](/wiki/Degenerated_workers%27_state "Degenerated workers' state") under the leadership of Stalin in which class relations had re-emerged in a new form. Trotsky's politics differed sharply from those of Stalin and Mao, most importantly in declaring the need for an international proletarian revolution – rather than socialism in one country – and support for a true dictatorship of the proletariat based on democratic principles. Struggling against Stalin for power in the Soviet Union, Trotsky and his supporters organized into the [Left Opposition](/wiki/Left_Opposition "Left Opposition"),[[240]](#cite_note-253) the platform of which became known as Trotskyism.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatenaude2017199-251)

In particular, Trotsky advocated for a [decentralised](/wiki/Decentralization "Decentralization") form of [economic planning](/wiki/Economic_planning "Economic planning"),[[241]](#cite_note-254) mass soviet [democratization](/wiki/Democratization "Democratization"),[[242]](#cite_note-255) elected representation of Soviet [socialist parties](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_the_Soviet_Union "List of political parties in the Soviet Union"),[[243]](#cite_note-256)[[244]](#cite_note-257) the tactic of a [united front](/wiki/United_front "United front") against far-right parties,[[245]](#cite_note-258)
[cultural](/wiki/Cultural "Cultural") autonomy for artistic movements,[[246]](#cite_note-259) voluntary [collectivisation](/wiki/Collectivisation "Collectivisation"),[[247]](#cite_note-260)[[248]](#cite_note-261) a [transitional program](/wiki/The_Death_Agony_of_Capitalism_and_the_Tasks_of_the_Fourth_International "The Death Agony of Capitalism and the Tasks of the Fourth International")[[249]](#cite_note-262) and socialist [internationalism](/wiki/Proletarian_internationalism "Proletarian internationalism").[[250]](#cite_note-263)

Trotsky had the support of many party [intellectuals](/wiki/Intellectuals "Intellectuals") but this was overshadowed by the huge apparatus which included the GPU and the party cadres who were at the disposal of Stalin.[[251]](#cite_note-264) Stalin eventually succeeded in gaining control of the Soviet regime and Trotskyist attempts to remove Stalin from power resulted in Trotsky's exile from the Soviet Union in 1929. While in exile, Trotsky continued his campaign against Stalin, founding in 1938 the [Fourth International](/wiki/Fourth_International "Fourth International"), a Trotskyist rival to the Comintern.[[252]](#cite_note-transitional-265)[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatenaude2017189,_194-266)[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnsonWalkerGray2014155Fourth_International_(FI)-267) In August 1940, Trotsky was assassinated in [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City") on Stalin's orders. Trotskyist currents include [orthodox Trotskyism](/wiki/Orthodox_Trotskyism "Orthodox Trotskyism"), [third camp](/wiki/Third_camp "Third camp"), [Posadism](/wiki/Posadism "Posadism"), and [Pabloism](/wiki/Pabloism "Pabloism").[[255]](#cite_note-268)[[256]](#cite_note-269)

The economic platform of a [planned economy](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy") combined with an authentic [worker's democracy](/wiki/Socialist_democracy "Socialist democracy") as originally advocated by Trotsky has constituted the programme of the Fourth International and the modern Trotskyist movement.[[257]](#cite_note-270)

##### Maoism

Main articles: [Maoism](/wiki/Maoism "Maoism") and [Marxism–Leninism–Maoism](/wiki/Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism%E2%80%93Maoism "Marxism–Leninism–Maoism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Mao_Statue_at_Zhong_Shan_Guang_Chang.jpg/250px-Mao_Statue_at_Zhong_Shan_Guang_Chang.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mao_Statue_at_Zhong_Shan_Guang_Chang.jpg)

[Long Live the Victory of Mao Zedong Thought](/wiki/Long_Live_the_Victory_of_Mao_Zedong_Thought "Long Live the Victory of Mao Zedong Thought") monument in Shenyang

Maoism is the theory derived from the teachings of the Chinese political leader [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong"). Developed from the 1950s until the [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping") [Chinese economic reform](/wiki/Chinese_economic_reform "Chinese economic reform") in the 1970s, it was widely applied as the guiding political and military ideology of the Communist Party of China and as the theory guiding [revolutionary movements](/wiki/Revolutionary_movement "Revolutionary movement") around the world. A key difference between Maoism and other forms of Marxism–Leninism is that [peasants](/wiki/Peasant "Peasant") should be the bulwark of the revolutionary energy which is led by the working class.[[258]](#cite_note-271) Three common Maoist values are revolutionary [populism](/wiki/Populism "Populism"), being practical, and [dialectics](/wiki/Dialectic "Dialectic").[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWomack2001-272)

The synthesis of Marxism–Leninism–Maoism,[[f]](#cite_note-273) which builds upon the two individual theories as the Chinese adaption of Marxism–Leninism, did not occur during the life of Mao. After [de-Stalinization](/wiki/De-Stalinization "De-Stalinization"), Marxism–Leninism was kept in the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), while certain [anti-revisionist](/wiki/Anti-revisionist "Anti-revisionist") tendencies like [Hoxhaism](/wiki/Hoxhaism "Hoxhaism") and Maoism stated that such had deviated from its original concept. Different policies were applied in Albania and China, which became more distanced from the Soviet Union. From the 1960s, groups who called themselves *Maoists*, or those who upheld Maoism, were not unified around a common understanding of Maoism, instead having their own particular interpretations of the political, philosophical, economical, and military works of Mao. Its adherents claim that as a unified, coherent higher stage of Marxism, it was not consolidated until the 1980s, first being formalized by the [Shining Path](/wiki/Shining_Path "Shining Path") in 1982.[[260]](#cite_note-On_Marxism-Leninism-Maoism-274) Through the experience of the [people's war](/wiki/People%27s_war "People's war") waged by the party, the Shining Path were able to posit Maoism as the newest development of Marxism.[[260]](#cite_note-On_Marxism-Leninism-Maoism-274)

#### Eurocommunism

Main article: [Eurocommunism](/wiki/Eurocommunism "Eurocommunism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Enrico_Berlinguer.jpg/250px-Enrico_Berlinguer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Enrico_Berlinguer.jpg)

[Enrico Berlinguer](/wiki/Enrico_Berlinguer "Enrico Berlinguer"), the secretary of the [Italian Communist Party](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party") and main proponent of Eurocommunism

Eurocommunism was a [revisionist](/wiki/Revisionism_(Marxism) "Revisionism (Marxism)") trend in the 1970s and 1980s within various Western European communist parties, claiming to develop a theory and practice of [social transformation](/wiki/Social_transformation "Social transformation") more relevant to their region. Especially prominent within the [French Communist Party](/wiki/French_Communist_Party "French Communist Party"), [Italian Communist Party](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party"), and [Communist Party of Spain](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Spain "Communist Party of Spain"), Communists of this nature sought to undermine the influence of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") and its [All-Union Communist Party (Bolsheviks)](/wiki/All-Union_Communist_Party_(Bolsheviks) "All-Union Communist Party (Bolsheviks)") during the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War").[[162]](#cite_note-Kindersley-169) Eurocommunists tended to have a larger attachment to liberty and democracy than their Marxist–Leninist counterparts.[[261]](#cite_note-275) [Enrico Berlinguer](/wiki/Enrico_Berlinguer "Enrico Berlinguer"), general secretary of Italy's major Communist party, was widely considered the father of Eurocommunism.[[262]](#cite_note-276)

### Libertarian Marxist communism

Main article: [Libertarian Marxism](/wiki/Libertarian_Marxism "Libertarian Marxism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Rosa_Luxemburg.jpg/250px-Rosa_Luxemburg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rosa_Luxemburg.jpg)

[Rosa Luxemburg](/wiki/Rosa_Luxemburg "Rosa Luxemburg") as a champion of socialist democracy who famously stated, "Freedom is always and exclusively freedom for the one who thinks differently."

Libertarian Marxism is a broad range of economic and political philosophies that emphasize the [anti-authoritarian](/wiki/Anti-authoritarian "Anti-authoritarian") aspects of [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism"). Early currents of libertarian Marxism, known as [left communism](/wiki/Left_communism "Left communism"),[[263]](#cite_note-277) emerged in opposition to Marxism–Leninism[[264]](#cite_note-Gorter_et_al._2007-278) and its derivatives such as [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") and [Maoism](/wiki/Maoism "Maoism"), as well as [Trotskyism](/wiki/Trotskyism "Trotskyism").[[265]](#cite_note-279) Libertarian Marxism is also critical of [reformist](/wiki/Reformist "Reformist") positions such as those held by [social democrats](/wiki/Social_democrats "Social democrats").[[266]](#cite_note-280) Libertarian Marxist currents often draw from Marx and Engels' later works, specifically the *[Grundrisse](/wiki/Grundrisse "Grundrisse")* and *[The Civil War in France](/wiki/The_Civil_War_in_France "The Civil War in France")*,[[267]](#cite_note-281) emphasizing the Marxist belief in the ability of the [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") to forge its own destiny without the need for a revolutionary party or [state](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") to mediate or aid its liberation.[[268]](#cite_note-282) Along with [anarchism](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism"), libertarian Marxism is one of the main derivatives of [libertarian socialism](/wiki/Libertarian_socialism "Libertarian socialism").[[269]](#cite_note-283)

Aside from left communism, libertarian Marxism includes such currents as [autonomism](/wiki/Autonomism "Autonomism"), [communization](/wiki/Communization "Communization"), [council communism](/wiki/Council_communism "Council communism"), [De Leonism](/wiki/De_Leonism "De Leonism"), the [Johnson–Forest Tendency](/wiki/Johnson%E2%80%93Forest_Tendency "Johnson–Forest Tendency"), [Lettrism](/wiki/Lettrism "Lettrism"), [Luxemburgism](/wiki/Luxemburgism "Luxemburgism"), [Situationism](/wiki/Situationism "Situationism"), [Socialisme ou Barbarie](/wiki/Socialisme_ou_Barbarie "Socialisme ou Barbarie"), [Solidarity](/wiki/Solidarity_(UK) "Solidarity (UK)"), the [World Socialist Movement](/wiki/World_Socialist_Movement "World Socialist Movement"), and [workerism](/wiki/Workerism "Workerism"), as well as parts of [Freudo-Marxism](/wiki/Freudo-Marxism "Freudo-Marxism"), and the [New Left](/wiki/New_Left "New Left").[[270]](#cite_note-284) Libertarian Marxism has often had a strong influence on both [post-left](/wiki/Post-left_anarchy "Post-left anarchy") and [social anarchists](/wiki/Social_anarchism "Social anarchism").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Notable theorists of libertarian Marxism have included [Anton Pannekoek](/wiki/Anton_Pannekoek "Anton Pannekoek"), [Raya Dunayevskaya](/wiki/Raya_Dunayevskaya "Raya Dunayevskaya"), [Cornelius Castoriadis](/wiki/Cornelius_Castoriadis "Cornelius Castoriadis"), [Maurice Brinton](/wiki/Maurice_Brinton "Maurice Brinton"), [Daniel Guérin](/wiki/Daniel_Gu%C3%A9rin "Daniel Guérin"), and [Yanis Varoufakis](/wiki/Yanis_Varoufakis "Yanis Varoufakis"),[[271]](#cite_note-285) the latter of whom claims that Marx himself was a libertarian Marxist.[[272]](#cite_note-286)

#### Council communism

Main article: [Council communism](/wiki/Council_communism "Council communism")

Council communism is a movement that originated from Germany and the Netherlands in the 1920s,[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnsonWalkerGray2014313–314Pannekoek,_Antonie_(1873–1960)-287) whose primary organization was the [Communist Workers' Party of Germany](/wiki/Communist_Workers%27_Party_of_Germany "Communist Workers' Party of Germany"). It continues today as a theoretical and activist position within both [libertarian Marxism](/wiki/Libertarian_Marxism "Libertarian Marxism") and [libertarian socialism](/wiki/Libertarian_socialism "Libertarian socialism").[[274]](#cite_note-288) The core principle of council communism is that the government and the economy should be managed by [workers' councils](/wiki/Workers%27_council "Workers' council"), which are composed of [delegates](/wiki/Delegate_model_of_representation "Delegate model of representation") elected at workplaces and [recallable](/wiki/Recall_election "Recall election") at any moment. Council communists oppose the perceived authoritarian and undemocratic nature of [central planning](/wiki/Central_planning "Central planning") and of [state socialism](/wiki/State_socialism "State socialism"), labelled [state capitalism](/wiki/State_capitalism "State capitalism"), and the idea of a revolutionary party,[[275]](#cite_note-The_New_Blanquism-289)[[276]](#cite_note-290) since council communists believe that a revolution led by a party would necessarily produce a [party dictatorship](/wiki/Party_dictatorship "Party dictatorship"). Council communists support a workers' democracy, produced through a federation of workers' councils.

In contrast to those of [social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy") and [Leninist](/wiki/Leninist "Leninist") communism, the central argument of council communism is that democratic workers' councils arising in the factories and municipalities are the natural forms of working-class organizations and governmental power.[[277]](#cite_note-291)[[278]](#cite_note-292) This view is opposed to both the [reformist](/wiki/Reformist "Reformist")[[279]](#cite_note-Socialism_and_Labor_Unionism-293) and the Leninist communist ideologies,[[275]](#cite_note-The_New_Blanquism-289) which respectively stress parliamentary and [institutional](/wiki/New_institutionalism "New institutionalism") government by applying [social reforms](/wiki/Reform_movement "Reform movement") on the one hand, and [vanguard parties](/wiki/Vanguard_parties "Vanguard parties") and [participative](/wiki/Participatory_democracy "Participatory democracy") [democratic centralism](/wiki/Democratic_centralism "Democratic centralism") on the other.[[279]](#cite_note-Socialism_and_Labor_Unionism-293)[[275]](#cite_note-The_New_Blanquism-289)

#### Left communism

Main article: [Left communism](/wiki/Left_communism "Left communism")

Left communism is the range of communist viewpoints held by the communist left, which criticizes the political ideas and practices espoused, particularly following the series of revolutions that brought [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") to an end by [Bolsheviks](/wiki/Bolsheviks "Bolsheviks") and [social democrats](/wiki/Social_democrats "Social democrats").[[280]](#cite_note-294) Left communists assert positions which they regard as more authentically [Marxist](/wiki/Marxist "Marxist") and [proletarian](/wiki/Proletarian "Proletarian") than the views of Marxism–Leninism espoused by the [Communist International](/wiki/Communist_International "Communist International") after its [first congress](/wiki/1st_Congress_of_the_Comintern "1st Congress of the Comintern") (March 1919) and during its [second congress](/wiki/2nd_World_Congress_of_the_Comintern "2nd World Congress of the Comintern") (July–August 1920).[[264]](#cite_note-Gorter_et_al._2007-278)[[281]](#cite_note-295)[[282]](#cite_note-296)

Left communists represent a range of political movements distinct from [Marxist–Leninists](/wiki/Marxist%E2%80%93Leninists "Marxist–Leninists"), whom they largely view as merely the left wing of [capital](/wiki/Capital_(economics) "Capital (economics)"), from [anarcho-communists](/wiki/Anarcho-communists "Anarcho-communists"), some of whom they consider to be [internationalist socialists](/wiki/Internationalist_socialists "Internationalist socialists"), and from various other revolutionary socialist tendencies, such as [De Leonists](/wiki/De_Leonists "De Leonists"), whom they tend to see as being internationalist socialists only in limited instances.[[283]](#cite_note-297) [Bordigism](/wiki/Bordigism "Bordigism") is a Leninist left-communist current named after [Amadeo Bordiga](/wiki/Amadeo_Bordiga "Amadeo Bordiga"), who has been described as being "more Leninist than Lenin", and considered himself to be a Leninist.[[284]](#cite_note-298)

### Other types of communism

#### Anarcho-communism

Main article: [Anarcho-communism](/wiki/Anarcho-communism "Anarcho-communism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Kropotkin2.jpg/250px-Kropotkin2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kropotkin2.jpg)

[Peter Kropotkin](/wiki/Peter_Kropotkin "Peter Kropotkin"), main theorist of [anarcho-communism](/wiki/Anarcho-communism "Anarcho-communism")

Anarcho-communism is a [libertarian](/wiki/Libertarian "Libertarian") theory of [anarchism](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism") and communism which advocates the abolition of the [state](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)"), [private property](/wiki/Private_property "Private property"), and [capitalism](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism") in favor of [common ownership](/wiki/Common_ownership "Common ownership") of the [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production");[[285]](#cite_note-Mayne-299)[[286]](#cite_note-300) [direct democracy](/wiki/Direct_democracy "Direct democracy"); and a [horizontal network](/wiki/Horizontal_integration "Horizontal integration") of [voluntary associations](/wiki/Voluntary_association "Voluntary association") and [workers' councils](/wiki/Workers%27_council "Workers' council") with production and consumption based on the guiding principle, "[From each according to his ability, to each according to his need](/wiki/From_each_according_to_his_ability,_to_each_according_to_his_needs "From each according to his ability, to each according to his needs")".[[287]](#cite_note-301)[[288]](#cite_note-302) Anarcho-communism differs from Marxism in that it rejects its view about the need for a [state socialism](/wiki/State_socialism "State socialism") phase prior to establishing communism. [Peter Kropotkin](/wiki/Peter_Kropotkin "Peter Kropotkin"), the main theorist of anarcho-communism, stated that a revolutionary society should "transform itself immediately into a communist society", that it should go immediately into what Marx had regarded as the "more advanced, completed, phase of communism".[[289]](#cite_note-theanarchistlibrary.org-303) In this way, it tries to avoid the reappearance of class divisions and the need for a state to be in control.[[289]](#cite_note-theanarchistlibrary.org-303)

Some forms of anarcho-communism, such as [insurrectionary anarchism](/wiki/Insurrectionary_anarchism "Insurrectionary anarchism"), are [egoist](/wiki/Egoist_anarchism "Egoist anarchism") and strongly influenced by radical [individualism](/wiki/Individualism "Individualism"),[[290]](#cite_note-304)[[291]](#cite_note-creativenothing-305)[[292]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-306) believing that anarchist communism does not require a [communitarian](/wiki/Communitarian "Communitarian") nature at all. Most anarcho-communists view anarchist communism as a way of reconciling the opposition between the individual and society.[[g]](#cite_note-307)[[293]](#cite_note-308)[[294]](#cite_note-309)

#### Christian communism

Main article: [Christian communism](/wiki/Christian_communism "Christian communism")

Christian communism is a theological and political theory based upon the view that the teachings of [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity") compel [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians") to support [religious communism](/wiki/Religious_communism "Religious communism") as the ideal [social system](/wiki/Social_system "Social system").[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELansford200724–25-54) Although there is no universal agreement on the exact dates when communistic ideas and practices in Christianity began, many Christian communists state that evidence from the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") suggests that the first Christians, including the [Apostles in the New Testament](/wiki/Apostles_in_the_New_Testament "Apostles in the New Testament"), established their own small communist society in the years following Jesus' death and resurrection.[[295]](#cite_note-310)

Many advocates of Christian communism state that it was taught by Jesus and practiced by the apostles themselves,[[296]](#cite_note-311) an argument that historians and others, including anthropologist Roman A. Montero,[[297]](#cite_note-312) scholars like [Ernest Renan](/wiki/Ernest_Renan "Ernest Renan"),[[298]](#cite_note-313)[[299]](#cite_note-314) and theologians like [Charles Ellicott](/wiki/Charles_Ellicott "Charles Ellicott") and [Donald Guthrie](/wiki/Donald_Guthrie_(theologian) "Donald Guthrie (theologian)"),[[300]](#cite_note-315)[[301]](#cite_note-316) generally agree with.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELansford200724–25-54)[[302]](#cite_note-317) Christian communism enjoys some support in Russia. Russian musician [Yegor Letov](/wiki/Yegor_Letov "Yegor Letov") was an outspoken Christian communist, and in a 1995 interview he was quoted as saying: "Communism is the [Kingdom of God](/wiki/Kingdom_of_God "Kingdom of God") on Earth."[[303]](#cite_note-318)

## Analysis

#### Achievements of the Soviet planned economies

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Sputnik-stamp-ussr.jpg/250px-Sputnik-stamp-ussr.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sputnik-stamp-ussr.jpg)

Soviet stamp showing the orbit of [Sputnik 1](/wiki/Sputnik_1 "Sputnik 1")

In the officially sanctioned textbooks describing the socialist planned economies as they existed in the 1980s, it was claimed as follows:

* Class and national oppression had been totally eradicated.
* [Unemployment](/wiki/Unemployment "Unemployment"), [hunger](/wiki/Hunger "Hunger"), [poverty](/wiki/Poverty "Poverty"), [illiteracy](/wiki/Illiteracy "Illiteracy") and uncertainty about the future had been eliminated.
* Every citizen had a guaranteed right to work, rest, [education](/wiki/Universal_education "Universal education"), [health care](/wiki/Universal_healthcare "Universal healthcare"), abode and security in old age and maintenance in the event of disability.
* Material standards of living were rising steadily and everyone had free access to knowledge and to the values of world and national culture.
* Every citizen had a right in practice to take part in discussing and solving any problems in the life of the enterprise, region, republic and the country they lived in, including the rights to free speech, of assembly and to demonstrate.[[304]](#cite_note-319)

Data collected by the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") of indicators of human development in the early 1990s show that a high level of social development was achieved in the former socialist planned economies of Central and Eastern Europe and the Commonwealth of Independent States (CEE/CIS). Life expectancy in the CEE/CIS area in the period 1985–1990 was 68 years, while for the countries of the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) it was 75 years.[[305]](#cite_note-320) Infant mortality in the CEE/CIS area was 25 for every 1,000 live births in 1990, compared to 13 in the OECD area.[[306]](#cite_note-321) In terms of education, the two areas enjoyed universal adult literacy and full enrollment of children in primary and secondary schools. For tertiary education, the CEE/CIS had 2,600 university students per 100,000 population, while in the OECD the comparable figure was 3,550 students. Overall enrollment at primary, secondary and tertiary levels was 75 percent in the CEE/CIS region and 82 percent in the OECD countries.[[307]](#cite_note-322)

The Soviet Union placed great emphasis on [science and technology](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_the_Soviet_Union "Science and technology in the Soviet Union").[[308]](#cite_note-323)[[309]](#cite_note-324) Lenin believed the USSR would never overtake the developed world if it remained as technologically backward as it was upon its founding. Soviet authorities proved their commitment to Lenin's belief by developing massive networks and research and development organizations. In the early 1960s, 40% of chemistry PhDs in the Soviet Union were attained by women, compared with only 5% in the United States.[[310]](#cite_note-325) By 1989, Soviet scientists were among the world's best-trained specialists in several areas, such as energy physics, selected areas of medicine, mathematics, welding, space technology, and military technologies. However, due to rigid state planning and [bureaucracy](/wiki/Nomenklatura "Nomenklatura"), the Soviets remained far behind the [First World](/wiki/First_World "First World") in chemistry, biology, and computer science. Under Stalin, the Soviet government persecuted [geneticists](/wiki/Geneticists "Geneticists") in favour of [Lysenkoism](/wiki/Lysenkoism "Lysenkoism"), a [pseudoscience](/wiki/Pseudoscience "Pseudoscience") rejected by the scientific community in the Soviet Union and abroad but supported by Stalin's inner circles. Implemented in the USSR and China, it resulted in reduced crop yields and is widely believed to have contributed to the [Great Chinese Famine](/wiki/Great_Chinese_Famine "Great Chinese Famine").[[311]](#cite_note-326) In the 1980s, the Soviet Union had more [scientists](/wiki/Scientists "Scientists") and [engineers](/wiki/Engineers "Engineers") relative to the world's population than any other major country, owing to strong levels of state support.[[312]](#cite_note-327) Some of its most remarkable technological achievements, such as launching the [world's first space satellite](/wiki/Sputnik_1 "Sputnik 1"), were achieved through military research.[[313]](#cite_note-Library_of_Congress_Country_Studies-328)

On housing the main problem was over-crowding rather than homelessness in the socialist planned economies. In the USSR the area of residential accommodation was 15.5 square meters per person by 1990 in urban areas but 15 percent of the population were without their own separate accommodation and had to live in communal apartments according to the 1989 census.[[314]](#cite_note-329) Housing was generally of good quality in both the CEE/CIS region and in the OECD countries: 98 and 99 percent of the population in the OECD countries had access to safe drinking water and improved sanitation respectively, compared to 93 and 85 percent in the CEE/CIS area by 1990.[[315]](#cite_note-330)

Unemployment did not exist officially in the socialist planned economies, though there were people between jobs and a fraction of unemployable people as a result of illness, disability or other problems, such as alcoholism. The proportion of people changing jobs was between 6 and 13 percent of the labour force a year according to employment data during the 1970s and 1980s in Central and Eastern Europe and the USSR. Labour exchanges were established in the USSR in 1967 to help enterprises re-allocate workers and provide information on job vacancies. Compulsory unemployment insurance schemes operated in Bulgaria, Eastern Germany and Hungary but the numbers claiming support as a result of losing their job through no fault of their own numbered a few hundred a year.[[316]](#cite_note-331)

By 1988, GDP per person, measured at purchasing power parity in US dollars, was $7,519 in Russia and $6,304 for the USSR. The highest income was to be found in Slovenia ($10,663) and Estonia ($9,078) and the lowest in Albania ($1,386) and Tajikistan ($2,730). Across the whole CEE/CIS area, GDP per person was estimated at $6,162.[[317]](#cite_note-332) This compared to the US with $20,651 and $16,006 for Germany in the same year. For the OECD area as a whole estimated GDP per person was $14,385.[[318]](#cite_note-333) Thus, on the basis of IMF estimates, national income (GDP) per person in the CEE/CIS area was 43 percent of that in the OECD area.

#### Economic problems of the Soviet planned economies

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/78/Graph_of_Soviet_National_Income_Growth.png/500px-Graph_of_Soviet_National_Income_Growth.png)](/wiki/File:Graph_of_Soviet_National_Income_Growth.png)

Soviet national income 1928–1987 growth in % based on estimates of the official statistical agency of the Soviet Union, the CIA and revised estimates by [Grigorii Khanin](/wiki/Grigorii_Khanin "Grigorii Khanin")

From the 1960s onwards, CMEA countries, beginning with East Germany, attempted "intensive" growth strategies, aiming to raise the productivity of labour and capital. However, in practice this meant that investment was shifted towards new branches of industry, including the electronics, computing, automotive and nuclear power sectors, leaving the traditional heavy industries dependent upon older technologies. Despite the rhetoric about modernization, innovation remained weak as enterprise managers preferred routine production that was easier to plan and brought them predictable bonuses. Embargoes on high technology exports organized through the US-supported [CoCom](/wiki/CoCom "CoCom") arrangement hampered technology transfer. Enterprise managers also ignored inducements to introduce labour-saving measures as they wished to retain a reserve of personnel to be available to meet their production target by working at top speed when supplies were delayed.[[319]](#cite_note-334)

Under conditions of "taut planning", the economy was expected to produce a volume of output higher than the reported capacity of enterprises and there was no "slack" in the system. Enterprises faced a resource constraint and hoarded labour and other inputs and avoided sub-contracting intermediate production activities, preferring to retain the work in-house. The enterprise, according to the theory promulgated by [János Kornai](/wiki/J%C3%A1nos_Kornai "János Kornai"), was constrained by its resources not by the demand for its goods and services; nor was it constrained by its finances since the government was not likely to shut it down if it failed to meet its financial targets. Enterprises in socialist planned economies operated within a "soft" budget constraint, unlike enterprises in capitalist market economies which are demand-constrained and operate within "hard" budget constraints, as they face bankruptcy if their costs exceed their sales. As all producers were working in a resource-constrained economy they were perpetually in short supply and the shortages could never be eliminated, leading to chronic disruption of production schedules. The effect of this was to preserve a high level of employment.[[320]](#cite_note-335)

As the supply of consumer goods failed to match rising incomes (because workers still received their pay even if they were not fully productive), household savings accumulated, indicating, in the official terminology, "postponed demand". Western economists called this "[monetary overhang](/wiki/Monetary_overhang "Monetary overhang")" or "repressed inflation". Prices on the black market were several times higher than in the official price-controlled outlets, reflecting the scarcity and possible illegality of the sale of these items. Therefore, although consumer welfare was reduced by shortages, the prices households paid for their regular consumption were lower than would have been the case had prices been set at market-clearing levels.[[321]](#cite_note-336)

Over the course of the 1980s it became clear that the CMEA area was "in crisis", although it remained viable economically and was not expected to collapse.[[322]](#cite_note-337) The "extensive" growth model was retarding growth in the CMEA as a whole, with member countries dependent upon supplies of raw materials from the USSR and upon the Soviet market for sales of goods. The decline in growth rates reflected a combination of diminishing returns to capital accumulation and low innovation as well as micro-economic inefficiencies, which a high rate of saving and investment was unable to counter. The CMEA was supposed to ensure coordination of national plans but it failed even to develop a common methodology for planning which could be adopted by its member states. As each member state was reluctant to give up national self-sufficiency the CMEA's efforts to encourage specialization was thwarted. There were very few joint ventures and therefore little intra-enterprise technology transfer and trade, which in the capitalist world was often undertaken by trans-national corporations. The International Bank for Economic Cooperation had no means of converting a country's trade surplus into an option to buy goods and services from other CMEA members.[[323]](#cite_note-338)
